The result presented in the main text is the case where Euclidean metric (PRESS) was used as a distance measure. Likewise, we also obtained small p\begin{document}$p$\end{document}-values with the other metrics (Appendix 1—table 2).

We could also estimate the proteomes with high accuracy using all the 14 dimensions of the LDA space (Appendix 1—table 3). As noted in ‘OLS in LOOCV scheme’ in Materials and methods, the regression is underdetermined in this case. Thus, we simply adopted the minimum-norm solution from among all least-squares solutions.

sec.title: Characterizing an SCG by analyzing the Raman-proteome correspondence matrix
Notation
sec.p: The component representation of Equation 2 is(3)(pi1pi2⋮pin)⏞p^i⊤=(b10b11⋯b1(m−1)b20b21⋯b2(m−1)⋮⋮⋱⋮bn0bn1⋯bn(m−1))⏞B(1ri1⋮ri(m−1))⏞[1r^i]⊤,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \overbrace{ \begin{pmatrix}\begin{matrix} p_{i1} \\ p_{i2} \\ {\vdots} \\p_{in} \end{matrix} \end{pmatrix}}^ {\hat{\boldsymbol{p}}_i^{\top}} = \overbrace{ \begin{pmatrix} b_{10} & b_{11} & \cdots & b_{1(m-1)} \\ b_{20} & b_{21} & \cdots & b_{2(m-1)} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ b_{n0} & b_{n1} & \cdots & b_{n(m-1)} \end{pmatrix} }^{B} \; \overbrace{ \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ r_{i1} \\ \vdots \\ r_{i(m-1)} \end{pmatrix} }^{\left[\,1 \;\; \hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_i\,\right]^{\top}}, $$\end{document}

where n\begin{document}$n$\end{document} is the number of proteins and m\begin{document}$m$\end{document} is the number of culture conditions. n=2058\begin{document}$n=2058$\end{document} and m=15\begin{document}$m=15$\end{document} in our case. Let 𝒃h-1\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{b}_{h-1}$\end{document} be the h\begin{document}$h$\end{document}-th row of th column of B\begin{document}$B$\end{document}. For example, 𝒃0=(b10⋯bn⁢0)⊤\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{b}_{0}=\matrixquantity(b_{10}&\cdots&b_{n0})^{\top}$\end{document} denotes the constant term for each protein, and 𝒃1=(b11⋯bn⁢1)⊤\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{b}_{1}=\matrixquantity(b_{11}&\cdots&b_{n1})^{\top}$\end{document} the coefficient of LDA1 for each protein. The expression level of protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} in the condition i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} is(4)pij=bj0+bj1ri1+…+bj(m−1)ri(m−1).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle p_{ij}=b_{j0}+b_{j1}r_{i1}+\ldots+b_{j(m-1)}r_{i(m-1)}.$$\end{document}

sec.title: Stoichiometry conservation of ISP COG class
sec.p: In the main text, we revealed that many proteins belonging to ISP COG class were aligned on a straight line passing through the origin when the relations between the columns of B\begin{document}$B$\end{document} were shown in scatterplots (Figure 3A). Consider hypothetical proteins that align perfectly on a straight line through the origin. Let e1,…,ek\begin{document}$e_{1},\ldots,e_{k}$\end{document} be the indices of such perfectly aligning protein species. Extracting only these rows for the proteins from Equation 3, we obtain(5)(pie1pie2⋮piek)=[b~0b~1⋯b~m−1](1ri1⋮ri(m−1))\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{pmatrix} p_{ie_1} \\ p_{ie_2} \\ \vdots \\p_{ie_k} \end{pmatrix}=\left[\begin{matrix} \tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}_{0} & \tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}_{1} & \cdots& \tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}_{m-1} \end{matrix}\right] \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ r_{i1} \\ \vdots\\ r_{i(m-1)} \end{pmatrix}$$\end{document}(6)=[b~0c1b~0⋯cm−1b~0](1ri1⋮ri(m−1))\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left[\begin{matrix} \tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}_{0} & c_1 \tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}_{0} & \cdots & c_{m-1} \tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}_{0}\end{matrix} \right]\begin{pmatrix}\begin{matrix} 1 \\ r_{i1} \\ \vdots \\ r_{i(m-1)} \end{matrix} \end{pmatrix}$$\end{document}(7)=(1+c1ri1+⋯+cm−1ri(m−1))b~0,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left(1+c_1r_{i1}+\cdots+c_{m-1}r_{i(m-1)}\right)\tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}_0,$$\end{document}

where ci\begin{document}$c_i$\end{document} (i=1,2,…,m-1\begin{document}$i=1,2,\ldots,m-1$\end{document}) are constants and b~h:=(be1h⋯bekh)⊤\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}_{h}:=\left( b_{e_{1}h}\;\cdots\; b_{e_{k}h } \right)^{\top}$\end{document}. For our data, Appendix 1—figure 4A and B correspond to Equation 7. In these plots, the y\begin{document}$y$\end{document}-axis represents 𝒑^i⊤\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{p}}_{i}^{\top}$\end{document}, and the x\begin{document}$x$\end{document}-axis 𝒃~0\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}_{0}$\end{document}. Many ISP proteins indeed align on a straight line through the origin with different slopes for different conditions (Appendix 1—figure 4A). In contrast, many proteins in other COG classes do not align on a straight line (Appendix 1—figure 4B).

Importantly, for a pair of proteins eα,eβ\begin{document}$e_{\alpha},e_{\beta}$\end{document} that align on the straight line,(8)pieαpieβ=beα0beβ0\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{p_{ie_{\alpha}}}{p_{ie_{\beta}}}=\frac{b_{e_{\alpha}0}}{b_{e_{\beta}0}}$$\end{document}

holds from Equation 7. The right-hand side of Equation 8 does not contain condition index i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}, which means that the abundance ratio of the proteins remains constant regardless of the conditions.

sec.title: On the evaluation of stoichiometry conservation by Pearson correlation coefficient
sec.p: In the main text, we used Pearson correlation coefficients to confirm the stoichiometry conservation of many ISP COG class members (Figure 3B). However, strictly speaking, cosine similarity is a more appropriate measure to evaluate stoichiometry conservation. In this analysis, cosine similarity can be written as(9)cos⁡θp^~ip^~j=p^~i⋅p^~j‖p^~i‖2‖p^~j‖2,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \cos\theta_{\tilde{\hat{\boldsymbol{p}}}_{i}\,\tilde{\hat{\boldsymbol{p}}}_{j}} = \frac{ \tilde{\hat{\boldsymbol{p}}}_{i}\cdot\tilde{\hat{\boldsymbol{p}}}_{j} }{ \left\lVert \tilde{\hat{\boldsymbol{p}}}_{i} \right\rVert_{2} \left\lVert \tilde{\hat{\boldsymbol{p}}}_{j} \right\rVert_{2} }, $$\end{document}

where 𝒑^~i\begin{document}$\tilde{\hat{\boldsymbol{p}}}_{i}$\end{document} and 𝒑^~j\begin{document}$\tilde{\hat{\boldsymbol{p}}}_{j}$\end{document} are the vectors representing the protein abundance for the proteome subgroups (‘ISP’ COG class, ‘Cellular processes and signaling’ COG class, and ‘Metabolism’ COG class) for conditions i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} and j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}, respectively (1≤i,j≤m\begin{document}$1\leq i,j\leq m$\end{document}). Cosine similarity version of Figure 3B is Appendix 1—figure 4F. The cosine similarity takes the maximum value 1 only when abundance ratios between all considered proteins are perfectly the same between the two compared conditions.
In addition, we also examined differences between COG classes by calculating Pearson correlation coefficients of log abundances (Appendix 1—figure 4E).

sec.title: Direct characterization of SCGs in omics data
Notation
sec.p: Let 𝒑i\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{i}$\end{document} be a column vector representing the abundances of protein i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}. Each component of this vector indicates the abundance of protein i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} under each condition. Therefore,(10)pi=(p1ip2i⋮pmi),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \boldsymbol{p}_{i}=\begin {pmatrix}\begin{matrix} p_{1i} \\ p_{2i} \\ \vdots \\ p_{mi} \end{matrix}\end{pmatrix} ,$$\end{document}

where m=15\begin{document}$m=15$\end{document} in our case. Note that 𝒑i\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{i}$\end{document} defined here is a 15-dimensional column vector and different from 𝒑^i\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{p}}_{i}$\end{document} introduced previously, which was a 2058-dimensional row vector.

sec.title: Identifying SCGs in omics data
sec.p: As explained in the main text, we extracted SCGs directly from the omics data, without referring to Raman data or COG classification. We evaluated the similarity of expression patterns for all the combinations of proteins using cosine similarity. Specifically, cosine similarity between proteins i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} and j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} is calculated as(11)cos⁡θpipj:=pi⋅pj‖pi‖2‖pj‖2.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}:= \frac{ \boldsymbol{p}_{i}\cdot\boldsymbol{p}_{j} }{ \lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{i} \rVert_{2} \lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2} }. $$\end{document}

This is the inner product of normalized 𝒑i\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{i}$\end{document} and 𝒑j\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{j}$\end{document}. Note that 0≤cos⁡θ𝒑i⁢𝒑j≤1\begin{document}$0\leq\cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\leq 1$\end{document} as protein abundances of any proteins are non-negative. cos⁡θ𝒑i⁢𝒑j\begin{document}$\cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}$\end{document} takes the maximum value 1 if and only if 𝒑i\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{i}$\end{document} and 𝒑j\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{j}$\end{document} point in identical direction, i.e., the abundance ratios of proteins i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} and j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} are constant under all the conditions. Therefore, if we extract only the proteins connected with high cosine similarity from all (n2)\begin{document}$\binom{n}{2}$\end{document} protein pairs, they would constitute proteome fractions in each of which the abundance ratios of the proteins remain almost constant across all the m\begin{document}$m$\end{document} conditions. We hence extracted only the protein pairs whose cosine similarity was above a high threshold of 0.995. As a result, we obtained several SCGs, in each of which the protein species are linked to each other with high cosine similarity (Figure 4B and C).

The genes in each SCG are listed in Appendix 1—table 4–8. Note that there are many other minor components (Figure 4B), some of which may have an expression pattern similar to another component but are separated due to the high threshold.
The positions of members of the SCGs on the chromosome are shown in Figure 4D (SCG 1 [homeostatic core]) and Appendix 1—figure 5E (SCGs 2–5).

sec.title: Global proteome structures based on stoichiometric balance
sec.p: In the previous section, we identified SCGs by setting a threshold of cosine similarity for extracting protein pairs. We next removed the threshold and considered the ‘distance’ with respect to cosine similarity for all the protein pairs to capture the global proteome structure that includes SCGs.

The cosine similarity for all the (n2)\begin{document}$\binom{n}{2}$\end{document} pairs of proteins can be summarized in one matrix as(12)A:=(cos⁡θpipj)1≤i,j≤n=(cos⁡θp1p1⋯cos⁡θp1pn⋮⋱⋮cos⁡θpnp1⋯cos⁡θpnpn),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle A := \left( \cos \theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_i \boldsymbol{p}_j} \right)_{1\leq i,j \leq n} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos \theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_1 \boldsymbol{p}_1} & \cdots & \cos \theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_1 \boldsymbol{p}_n} \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \cos \theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_n \boldsymbol{p}_1} & \cdots & \cos \theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_n \boldsymbol{p}_n} \end{pmatrix}, $$\end{document}

where (i,j\begin{document}$i,j$\end{document}) component represents cosine similarity between proteins i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} and j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}. Assuming that this matrix is an adjacency matrix in graph theory and network theory, the entire proteomes are considered as a weighted undirected complete graph (with loops), where nodes correspond to protein types and any protein pair is connected by an undirected edge. Each edge is weighted by the cosine similarity between the two protein species at both ends. Note that all the diagonal elements of A\begin{document}$A$\end{document} are one, which represents that each node has a loop with weight of one. These were introduced just for simplicity.

To ask whether the SCGs identified in the previous section have any unique features in this network, we evaluated the degree to which each node is central in the network structure. In graph theory, ‘centrality’ is known as an index to measure how ‘important’ or ‘influential’ each node is. In particular, we employed a measure called ‘degree centrality’ (for weighted graphs) (Nieminen, 1973; Segarra and Ribeiro, 2015). Degree centrality, which is also called ‘degree’, simply measures ‘influence’ of a node on a network on the basis of links with its direct neighborhood. One can obtain a degree centrality value by calculating the sum of the weights of all the edges connected to each node (see also the definition of the degree matrix D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} in Equation 14 below). We note that in our graph, degree centrality vector A⁢𝟏n(=D⁢𝟏n)\begin{document}$A\boldsymbol{1}_{n}(=D\boldsymbol{1}_{n})$\end{document}, where 𝟏n\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{1}_{n}$\end{document} is an n\begin{document}$n$\end{document}-dimensional column vector of which all elements are one, is equal to the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue of a ‘normalized’ adjacency matrix (D-1⁢A)⊤=A⁢D-1\begin{document}$(D^{-1}A)^{\top}=AD^{-1}$\end{document} up to multiplication by a constant. From this perspective, the centrality index we adopted measures ‘influence’ of a node in a recursive manner depending on ‘influence’ of its neighboring nodes. A well-known example of this centrality indicator is Google’s PageRank (Brin and Page, 1998) used for ranking web pages on the World Wide Web. It can be regarded as a variant of ‘eigenvector centrality’ (the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue of the adjacency matrix A\begin{document}$A$\end{document}) (Bonacich, 1972; Segarra and Ribeiro, 2015). As explained in the main text, the protein species in the homeostatic core (the largest SCG) had high centrality scores, while those in the other condition-specific SCGs had low centrality scores (Figure 5A).

We directly observed the global stoichiometry conservation structure of this proteome graph using Laplacian eigenmaps (Figures 5K, L, 6D). In general, a graph can be uniquely specified not only by the adjacency matrix A\begin{document}$A$\end{document}, but also by the Laplacian matrix L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} defined as(13)L:=D−A,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle L:= D-A,$$\end{document}

where D=(di⁢j)\begin{document}$D=\quantity(d_{ij})$\end{document} is the degree matrix with the components of(14)dij={(A1n)i(i=j)0(i≠j),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle d_{ij}=\begin{cases} (A\boldsymbol{1}_{n})_{i} &(i=j)\\0&(i\neq j) \end{cases},$$\end{document}

where 𝟏n\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{1}_{n}$\end{document} is an n\begin{document}$n$\end{document}-dimensional column vector of which all elements are one. The (i,i)\begin{document}$(i,i)$\end{document}-element of D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} represents the sum of the weights of all the edges connected to node i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}. In our case, it represents the sum of cosine similarity values between protein i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} and the other proteins. To see the entire proteome graph structure, we specifically employed the normalized Laplacian,(15)Lrw=D-1⁢L=I-D-1⁢A.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle L_{\mathrm{rw}}=D^{-1}L=I-D^{-1}A.$$\end{document}

We remark that there are two types of often-used normalized Laplacian matrices, Lrw\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document} and Lsym=D-1/2⁢L⁢D-1/2=I-D-1/2⁢A⁢D-1/2\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}=D^{-1/2}LD^{-1/2}=I-D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}$\end{document}, in the field of machine learning (von Luxburg, 2007), and our mathematical analysis can provide a clear interpretation to each of them in the context of the Raman-proteome linear correspondence as described in Appendix 2.1.5.

There exist m-1\begin{document}$m-1$\end{document} nontrivial eigenvalues of Lrw\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document} that are greater than zero and less than one. We write these m-1\begin{document}$m-1$\end{document} eigenvalues as λLE1,…,λLE⁢(m-1)\begin{document}$\lambda_{\mathrm{LE}1},\ldots,\lambda_{\mathrm{LE}(m-1)}$\end{document} from the smallest and the corresponding eigenvectors as 𝒗rw,1,…,𝒗rw,(m-1)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{v}_{\mathrm{rw},1},\ldots,\boldsymbol{v}_{\mathrm{rw},(m-1)}$\end{document}. Additionally, we denote the eigenvector corresponding to the eigenvalue zero as 𝒗rw,0\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{v}_{\mathrm{rw},0}$\end{document}. Using these eigenvectors, one can construct a matrix V~rw=[vrw,0vrw,1⋯vrw,(m−1)]\begin{document}$\begin{array}{ll}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}=\left[\begin{matrix}\boldsymbol{v}_{\mathrm{rw},0}& \boldsymbol{v}_{\mathrm{rw},1}&\cdots&\boldsymbol{v}_{\mathrm{rw},(m-1)}\end{matrix}\right]\end{array}$\end{document} and visualize a proteome, assigning protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} with a coordinate specified by the elements after the second column in the j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}-th row of V~rw\begin{document}$\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document}, i.e., by the j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}-th row of [vrw,1⋯vrw,(m−1)]\begin{document}$\left[\begin{matrix}\boldsymbol{v}_{\mathrm{rw},1}&\cdots&\boldsymbol{v}_{\mathrm{ rw},(m-1)}\end{matrix}\right]$\end{document}. The csLE structure we illustrate in the main figures was produced by using these eigenvectors. For example, the csLE1-csLE2 figure in the main text (Figure 6D) is a scatterplot between 𝒗rw,1\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{v}_{\mathrm{rw},1}$\end{document} and 𝒗rw,2\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{v}_{\mathrm{rw},2}$\end{document}. Note that the closer to one the cosine similarity of a protein pair is (the more similar their expression patterns are), the ‘closer’ the two protein species are placed (see Section 2.1.5 in Appendix for details).

This method of obtaining low-dimensional representation of data using eigenvectors of a graph Laplacian is known as Laplacian eigenmaps (LE) (Belkin and Niyogi, 2001; Belkin and Niyogi, 2003). Thus, what we explained above is the LE of a graph with edges weighted with cosine similarity of expression patterns of nodes (protein species). It differs from the original and common usages of LE in that the graph we considered is a complete graph (with loops) and that the weight of edges (pairwise similarity of nodes) is cosine similarity. It has made all the mathematical formulations linear, which allowed us to biologically interpret the results with mathematically rigorous analyses. We also remark that our graph representation of proteome does not rely on existing knowledge on the underlying interaction and regulatory networks of proteins and is based only on final expression levels of the proteins. Therefore, the results are robust against the uncertainty of underlying molecular detail.

sec.title: Relevance of centrality of csLE structure to biological functions
Centrality-essentiality correlation
sec.p: As mentioned in the main text, centrality of protein species with regard to stoichiometry conservation correlates with gene essentiality (Figure 5B). We analyzed the proteome data from all the 22 conditions reported by Schmidt et al., 2016 in Figure 5B. Interestingly, the centrality-essentiality correlation becomes weaker when the analysis was conducted with the data from fewer conditions (Appendix 1—figure 6A).

We obtained the list of essential genes of E. coli from EcoCyc (Keseler et al., 2017) on September 23, 2020. The list contained 318 essential genes in total. The essentiality of the genes in this list was determined on the basis of whether single-gene knockouts of BW25113 (Keio Collection) could grow under LB condition at 37°C (Baba et al., 2006).

We also confirmed centrality-essentiality correlation for S. pombe transcriptome data (Kobayashi-Kirschvink et al., 2018; Appendix 1—figure 6B, see Appendix 3.2). For this analysis, we downloaded the list of essential genes of S. pombe from PomBase (Harris et al., 2022) on May 13, 2022. The list contained 1221 essential genes in total. Here, the essentiality data by PomBase was based on the Fission Yeast Phenotype Ontology terms ‘inviable vegetative cell population’ (FYPO:0002061) and ‘viable vegetative cell population’ (FYPO:0002060) (Harris et al., 2013). Note that in our S. pombe essentiality analysis, we focused only on coding genes, whereas the csLE structure was calculated using both coding and non-coding genes. See ‘Centrality-coding/non-coding correlation’ in Materials and methods and Appendix 1—figure 6C for the proportion of coding genes in each bin in Appendix 1—figure 6B. Eleven coding genes in the S. pombe transcriptome data were not found in current PomBase. Thus, some bins do not show 100% in total in Appendix 1—figure 6B.

Stoichiometry conservation centrality in human cells was evaluated using two kinds of H. sapiens transcriptome data: (i) human cell atlas data reported in Cao et al., 2020 (Figure 5F) and (ii) genome-wide Perturb-seq data reported in Replogle et al., 2022 (Figure 5H).

The human cell atlas data (Cao et al., 2020) contain gene expression profiles in cells from 15 fetal organs. To calculate stoichiometry conservation centrality from the human cell atlas, we analyzed the pseudobulk data (GSE156793_S4_gene_expression_tissue.txt provided at the Gene Expression Omnibus). We calculated stoichiometry conservation centrality value of each gene using expression level data of 53,908 genes that are expressed at least in one organ.

The Perturb-seq data we used are gene expression profiles in a chronic myeloid leukemia cell line K562 (Replogle et al., 2022). This dataset contains single-cell RNA sequencing data of genetically perturbed cells in which expression of targeted genes is inhibited by CRISPR interference. We analyzed the pseudobulk data (K562_gwps_raw_bulk_01.h5ad provided at Figshare) to calculate stoichiometry conservation centrality. We evaluated stoichiometry conservation centrality value of each gene using the expression data of all the 8248 genes in the Perturb-seq data. We remark that this dataset did not contain genes that showed no expression under all the reported genetic perturbation conditions.

Human gene essentiality was determined by referring to another dataset reported in Wang et al., 2015, in which fitness cost imposed by gene inactivation was evaluated by a CRISPR-based method (Wang et al., 2015). The fitness cost was quantified by an index called CRISPR score; genes with lower CRISPR scores are considered more essential (Wang et al., 2015). We used the CRISPR scores calculated with a human chronic myelogenous leukemia cell line KBM7.

The CRISPR scores of 16,996 genes and 7462 genes were found in Wang et al., 2015 among the genes whose stoichiometry conservation centrality was evaluated using the human cell atlas data (Cao et al., 2020) and the Perturb-seq data (Replogle et al., 2022), respectively. We evaluated the correlations between stoichiometry conservation centrality and gene essentiality (CRISPR scores) for these common genes in Figure 5F and H. The correlations were examined with the Brunner-Munzel test (Brunner and Munzel, 2000) using R (version 4.0.3) and ‘brunnermunzel’ package (version 2.0) (Ara, 2022).

sec.title: Centrality-evolutionary conservation correlation
sec.p: As mentioned in the main text, centrality of proteins with regard to expression stoichiometry conservation weakly correlates with evolutionary conservation represented by the number of orthologs based on protein sequences (Figure 5C). In Figure 5C, we analyzed the proteome data from all the 22 conditions reported in Schmidt et al., 2016. We also confirmed the relation for the E. coli proteome data from fewer conditions which we had used for our Raman-proteome correspondence analyses (Appendix 1—figure 6D).

We obtained the ortholog data from OrthoMCL-DB (Chen et al., 2006) (release 6.12). We used the number of orthologs in all of the ‘Core species’ and the ‘Peripheral species’ of OrthoMCL, which are across the three domains (Bacteria, Archaea, and Eukaryota), as a proxy for evolutionary conservation of each protein. To examine the correlation, we performed the Brunner-Munzel test (Brunner and Munzel, 2000) using R (version 4.0.3) and ‘brunnermunzel’ package (version 2.0) (Ara, 2022). The E. coli proteome data contain 15 proteins with IDs that were not found in OrthoMCL-DB for technical reasons such as changes in IDs in the past, and thus, we manually processed these 15 proteins.

We also examined S. pombe transcriptome data (Kobayashi-Kirschvink et al., 2018; Appendix 1—figure 6E–G, see Appendix 3.2). We obtained ortholog data from OrthoMCL-DB (Chen et al., 2006) (release 6.12). The S. pombe transcriptome data have 11 coding genes which were not found in both current PomBase and OrthoMCL-DB, and two coding genes which were found in PomBase but not in OrthoMCL-DB. The S. pombe transcriptome data contain not only coding genes but also non-coding genes, and we obtained the csLE structure using both.

We also evaluated stoichiometry conservation-evolutionary conservation correlation using the human cell atlas data (Cao et al., 2020; Figure 5G) and the genome-wide Perturb-seq data (Replogle et al., 2022; Figure 5I). Ortholog data for these analyses were obtained from OrthoMCL-DB (release 6.20). We found the ortholog data in OrthoMCL-DB for 18,959 genes among the 53,908 genes with stoichiometry conservation centrality evaluated with the human cell atlas data. We remark that 98.7% of the 18,959 genes were classified as coding genes in the human cell atlas data. We also found the ortholog data for 7957 genes among the 8248 genes with stoichiometry conservation centrality evaluated with the Perturb-seq data. The correlations were examined with the Brunner-Munzel test (Brunner and Munzel, 2000) using R (version 4.0.3) and ‘brunnermunzel’ package (version 2.0) (Ara, 2022).

sec.title: Centrality-coding/non-coding correlation
sec.p: As mentioned in the main text and ‘Centrality-essentiality correlation’ in Materials and methods, centrality of genes with regard to stoichiometry conservation clearly correlates with coding/non-coding classification of genes in S. pombe. We observed this trend using S. pombe transcriptome data (Kobayashi-Kirschvink et al., 2018; Appendix 1—figure 6C). The coding/non-coding assignment of each gene is based on PomBase (Harris et al., 2022) data downloaded on October 11, 2022.

We observed a comparable correlation even in the human cell atlas data (Figure 5E). The gene type assignment is based on the human cell atlas data. Note that almost all the genes in the Perturb-seq data were coding genes.

sec.title: Global omics structures characterized by Raman-omics correspondences
Notation
sec.p: Let b^j\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{j}$\end{document} denote the j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}-th row in B\begin{document}$B$\end{document} (see Equation 3). It is an m\begin{document}$m$\end{document}-dimensional row vector whose components represent coefficients of protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}. The first component is the constant term, and the i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}-th component is the coefficient for LDA(i-1\begin{document}$i-1$\end{document}) Raman. Below, we consider the coefficients normalized with the constant terms,(16)b^jnorm:=(1bj1bj0⋯bj(m−1)bj0).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle {\boldsymbol{\hat b}}^{\mathrm{norm}}_{j} := \left(\begin{matrix} 1 & \frac{b_{j1}}{b_{j0}} & \cdots & \frac{b_{j(m-1)}}{b_{j0}} \end{matrix}\right). $$\end{document}

sec.title: Raman-proteome correspondence matrix as a low-dimensional representation of proteome changes
sec.p: We asked whether the stoichiometry conservation structure of the proteomes revealed by LE (Figures 5K, L, 6D) is relevant to the low-dimensional Raman LDA space. To address this, we focused on a proteome low-dimensional structure specified by the Raman-proteome coefficients, motivated by the fact that the analysis of B\begin{document}$B$\end{document} led to the discovery of a proteome fraction that conserves mutual stoichiometry (Figure 3). We considered a space where 𝒃^jnorm\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{j}^{\mathrm{norm}}$\end{document} represents the coordinate of each protein. From Equation 7 or Equation 8, protein species whose abundance ratios remain constant have an identical coordinate in this normalized coefficient space. The proteome in this normalized coefficient space is shown in Figure 6A and C.

This structure (Figure 6A and C) is constructed using the Raman LDA axes (dual basis) and is different from the csLE structure (Figures 5K, L, 6D), which is independent of Raman information. Therefore, it is nontrivial that these two structures are similar. This similarity suggests that differences in cellular Raman spectra captured by LDA might be quantitatively related to the omics structure deduced from stoichiometry-conserving relations. We will mathematically analyze this similarity in Section 2 in Appendix.

sec.title: Evaluating similarity between orthogonal matrix Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} and identity matrix
sec.p: As we see in Appendix 2.1.5, an orthogonal matrix Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} that appears in the relation connecting the two types of proteome structure must be close to an identity matrix to guarantee the structural similarity. To evaluate to what extent Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} is close to an identity matrix, we generated many random orthogonal matrices (Mezzadri, 2006) and compared Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} and the identity matrix with them.

We first multiplied each orthogonal matrix by itself in the sense of Hadamard product (element-wise product). Then, we regarded the resultant matrix as a scatterplot (Appendix 1—figure 9B) and calculated its Pearson correlation coefficient, assuming that (i,j)\begin{document}$(i,j)$\end{document} element was the frequency of ‘data points’ at the coordinate (i,j)\begin{document}$(i,j)$\end{document}. The obtained Pearson correlation coefficient can be regarded as a measure of closeness to the identity matrix. In the case of the identity matrix, the correlation coefficient takes the maximum value, one, because non-zero values are concentrated on the diagonal part.

We calculated the square of each matrix in the sense of Hadamard product for two reasons. First, since all elements of the resultant matrix are non-negative, one can ensure that the number of ‘points’ is non-negative at any coordinate. Note that it is not necessarily an integer here. Second, the sum of all the elements of the resultant matrix is necessarily m\begin{document}$m$\end{document}. Thus, the total number of ‘points’ is equally m\begin{document}$m$\end{document} for any m×m\begin{document}$m\times m$\end{document} orthogonal matrices compared.

In addition to this method, we also evaluated the closeness of Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} to the identity matrix (i) by comparing the magnitudes of off-diagonal elements among Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document}, the identity matrix, and random orthogonal matrices, and (ii) by comparing the magnitudes of elements of leading principal submatrices among Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document}, the identity matrix, and random orthogonal matrices. In (i), from a part consisting of (m-1)\begin{document}$(m-1)$\end{document}- and -(m-1)\begin{document}$-(m-1)$\end{document}-diagonals ((1,m)\begin{document}$(1,m)$\end{document} and (m,1)\begin{document}$(m,1)$\end{document} elements) to the whole matrix, we expand step by step the area to consider by including i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}- and -i\begin{document}$-i$\end{document}-diagonals (m-1≥i≥0\begin{document}$m-1\geq i\geq 0$\end{document}, the final step is inclusion of the main diagonal), and calculated the sum of the square of the elements in the area at each step. In (ii), from the smallest leading principal submatrix ((1,1)\begin{document}$(1,1)$\end{document} element) to the whole matrix, we expand the area to consider step by step and calculate the sum of the square of the elements in the area at each step. See also schematic diagrams in the figures in Appendix (e.g. Appendix 1—figure 9D and E).

sec.title: Interpretation of L1\begin{document}$L^{1}$\end{document} norm/L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} norm ratio of an expression vector as a quantitative measure of expression generality
sec.p: In Appendix 2.1.5, we will also see that even if Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} is close to the identity matrix, there is another condition which must be met to guarantee the similarity of the two types of proteome structure. By considering the mathematics behind the condition, we will reveal that the two indices, stoichiometry conservation centrality (degree) dj=∑icos⁡θ𝒑i⁢𝒑j\begin{document}$d_{j}=\sum_{i}\cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}$\end{document} and expression generality score gj=‖pj‖1/‖pj‖2\begin{document}$g_{j}=\left.\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\right\|_{1} \big/\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\right\|_{2} \right.$\end{document}, must be mutually proportional. Here, we explain why gj\begin{document}$g_j$\end{document} is a quantitative measure of the generality (or constancy) of expression levels.

First, we note that the ratio ‖pj‖1/‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\left.\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\right\| _{1}\big/\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\right\| _{2}\right.$\end{document} is independent of the magnitude of the expression vector 𝒑j\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{j}$\end{document}. In other words, normalization does not affect the ratio:(17)‖pj‖pj‖2‖1‖pj‖pj‖2‖2=∑i=1m|pij|‖pj‖2∑i=1m(pij)2‖pj‖22=‖pj‖1‖pj‖2‖pj‖2‖pj‖2=‖pj‖1‖pj‖2.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{ \left\lVert \dfrac{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}} \right\rVert_{1} }{ \left\lVert \dfrac{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}} \right\rVert_{2} } = \frac{ \sum\limits_{i=1}^{m} \dfrac{\lvert p_{ij} \rvert}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}} }{ \sqrt{ \sum\limits_{i=1}^{m} \dfrac{(p_{ij})^{2}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}^{2}} } } = \frac{ \dfrac{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{1}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}} }{ \dfrac{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}} } = \frac{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{1}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}}. $$\end{document}

On the basis of this, we only consider normalized expression vectors pj/‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\left.\boldsymbol{p}_{j}\big/\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\right\| _{2}\right.$\end{document} without loss of generality.

By definition, L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} norm of a normalized expression vector (the denominator of the most left-hand side of Equation 17) equals one. Thus, the ratio we are considering equals the L1\begin{document}$L^{1}$\end{document} norm of the normalized expression vector:(18)‖pj‖1‖pj‖2=‖pj‖pj‖2‖1.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{1}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}} = \left\lVert \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}} \right\rVert_{1}. $$\end{document}

Here, we write(19)pj‖pj‖2=(p~1jp~2j⋮p~mj),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}} = \begin{pmatrix} \tilde{p}_{1j} \\ \tilde{p}_{2j} \\ \vdots \\ \tilde{p}_{mj} \end{pmatrix},$$\end{document}

where p~1j,p~2j,…,p~mj≥0\begin{document}$\tilde{p}_{1j},\tilde{p}_{2j},\ldots,\tilde{p}_{mj}\geq 0$\end{document}. Then,(20)‖pj‖1‖pj‖2=‖pj‖pj‖2‖1=∑i=1mp~ij.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{1}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}} = \left\lVert \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}} \right\rVert_{1} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \tilde{p}_{ij}. $$\end{document}

Note that ∑i=1m(p~i⁢j)2=1\begin{document}$\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\tilde{p}_{ij}\right)^{2}=1$\end{document} holds because of normalization. Therefore, any normalized expression vector corresponds to a point on the first orthant division of the unit (m-1)\begin{document}$(m-1)$\end{document}-sphere ∑i=1m(xi)2=1 (x1,…,xm≥0)\begin{document}$\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(x_{i}\right)^{2}=1\ (x_{1},\ldots,x_{m}\geq 0)$\end{document}.

Next, we consider a hyperplane ∑i=1mxi=k\begin{document}$\sum_{i=1}^{m}x_{i}=k$\end{document} which passes through the point in Equation 19. Since all the coefficients of this hyperplane are equal, all the m\begin{document}$m$\end{document} intercepts are also equal. The intercept value is k=∑i=1mp~i⁢j\begin{document}$k=\sum_{i=1}^{m}\tilde{p}_{ij}$\end{document}, thus equals the ratio in Equation 20. In other words, the ratio from Equation 20 appears as an intercept of the hyperplane passing through the point corresponding to the normalized vector pj/‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\left.\boldsymbol{p}_{j}\big/\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\right\| _{2}\right.$\end{document} with all the coefficients equal to one.

By simple calculation, one can see that the two surfaces ∑i=1m(xi)2=1⁢(x1,…,xm≥0)\begin{document}$\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(x_{i}\right)^{2}=1\ (x_{1},\ldots,x_{m}\geq 0)$\end{document} and ∑i=1mxi=k\begin{document}$\sum_{i=1}^{m}x_{i}=k$\end{document} intersect when 1≤k≤m\begin{document}$1\leq k\leq\sqrt{m}$\end{document}. (In other words, ‖pj‖2≤‖pj‖1≤m‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\right\| _{2}\leq\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\right\| _{1}\leq\sqrt{m} \left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\right\| _{2}$\end{document} holds.) The intercept value k=∑i=1mp~i⁢j\begin{document}$k=\sum_{i=1}^{m}\tilde{p}_{ij}$\end{document} takes the maximum k=m\begin{document}$k=\sqrt{m}$\end{document} when the normalized expression vector points to the ‘center’ of the first orthant division of the unit (m-1)\begin{document}$(m-1)$\end{document}-sphere, i.e., when(21)pj‖pj‖2=(p~1jp~2j⋮p~mj)=1m(11⋮1).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}} = \begin{pmatrix} \tilde{p}_{1j} \\ \tilde{p}_{2j} \\ \vdots \\ \tilde{p}_{mj} \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{m}} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ \vdots \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}. $$\end{document}

This means that the expression level is even and constant across the conditions. When this evenness of expression level breaks, the intercept value k\begin{document}$k$\end{document} decreases, and it attains the minimum k=1\begin{document}$k=1$\end{document} when the normalized expression vector overlaps with an axis, i.e., when(22)pj‖pj‖2=(p~1j⋮p~(μ−1)jp~μjp~(μ+1)j⋮p~mj)=(0⋮010⋮0),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}{\lVert \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \rVert_{2}} = \begin{pmatrix} \tilde{p}_{1j} \\ \vdots \\ \tilde{p}_{(\mu-1)j} \\ \tilde{p}_{\mu j} \\ \tilde{p}_{(\mu+1)j} \\ \vdots \\ \tilde{p}_{mj} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{pmatrix},$$\end{document}
which corresponds to a completely ‘condition-specific expression pattern’ (μ is the condition’s index).
See Appendix 1—figure 8A and B for a graphical explanation of the argument for the two- and three-dimensional cases.

sec.title: Proteome structure obtained with PCA
sec.p: As mentioned in the main text, we confirmed that PCA could find a proteome structure (Appendix 1—figure 6H) similar to the csLE structure (Figure 5L and Figure 6D). Since cosine similarity of expression vectors is inner product of the L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document}-normalized expression vectors, we also performed L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} normalization of proteome data before applying PCA in this analysis. In other words, we applied PCA to a normalized proteome data [p1/‖p1‖2⋯pn/‖pn‖2]\begin{document}$\left[\boldsymbol{p}_{1}\big/ \left\|\boldsymbol{p}_{1}\right\|_2 \cdots \boldsymbol{p}_{n}\big/\left\|\boldsymbol{p}_{n}\right\|_2 \right] $\end{document} (see Appendix 2.1.5).

We remark that, despite the structural similarity between the PCA structure and the csLE structure, csLE has an advantage over PCA in that the relative proximity of positions reflects the strength of stoichiometry conservation between each element. In addition, as shown in the main text and Section 2 in Appendix, csLE of omics data has a direct quantitative connection to cellular Raman spectra, which is not the case for PCA.

sec.p: All data and analysis codes have been deposited in Zenodo and are publicly available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17090710.

Absolute quantification of protein and mRNA abundances demonstrate variability in gene-specific translation efficiency in yeast
Evolutionary convergence of pathway-specific enzyme expression stoichiometry
First-principles model of optimal translation factors stoichiometry
Network-based methods for predicting essential genes or proteins: a survey
How to generate random matrices from the classical compact groups
Studies on the role of ribonucleic acid in the growth of bacteria
On the centrality in a directed graph
Feedback regulation of ribosomal protein gene expression in Escherichia coli: structural homology of ribosomal RNA and ribosomal protein mRNA
Permutation P-values should never be zero: calculating exact P-values when permutations are randomly drawn

element-citation.article-title: Mapping information-rich genotype-phenotype landscapes with genome-scale Perturb-seq
Autoregulation of yeast ribosomal proteins discovered by efficient search for feedback regulation

Smoothing and differentiation of data by simplified least squares procedures
The quantitative and condition-dependent Escherichia coli proteome
Absolute proteome composition and dynamics during dormancy and resuscitation of Mycobacterium tuberculosis
Interdependence of cell growth and gene expression: origins and consequences
Module networks: identifying regulatory modules and their condition-specific regulators from gene expression data
Stability and continuity of centrality measures in weighted graphs
Physiological studies of Escherichia coli strain MG1655: growth defects and apparent cross-regulation of gene expression
Inferring time derivatives including cell growth rates using Gaussian processes
A genomic perspective on protein families
The COG database: an updated version includes eukaryotes
Visualizing data using t-SNE
A tutorial on spectral clustering
Canalization of development and genetic assimilation of acquired characters

sec.title: 1 Materials and methods
sec.p: See Materials and methods section in the main text.

sec.title: 2 Mathematical analysis and details
sec.p: To clarify what is nontrivial in the correspondence between LDA Raman and csLE proteome, here we derive rigorous mathematical relations for the correspondence through linear algebraic calculation.

sec.title: 2.1.1 Notations
sec.p: We use the following notations:

list-item.p: 1x\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{1}_{x}$\end{document} denotes an x\begin{document}$x$\end{document}-dimensional column vector of ones, and 0x\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{0}_{x}$\end{document} does an x\begin{document}$x$\end{document}-dimensional zero column vector.

A vector without a hat (e.g. x\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{x}$\end{document}) is a column vector, and a vector with a hat (e.g. x^\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}$\end{document}) is a row vector.
I\begin{document}$I$\end{document} denotes an identity matrix, and O\begin{document}$O$\end{document} does a zero matrix.

For a square matrix X=(xij)\begin{document}$X = \left(x_{ij}\right)$\end{document}, diag(X)=(δijxij)\begin{document}$\mathrm{diag}(X) = \left(\delta_{ij}x_{ij}\right)$\end{document}, and for a vector x=(xi)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{x}= (x_{i})$\end{document}, diag(x)=(δijxi)\begin{document}$\mathrm{diag}(\boldsymbol{x}) = \left(\delta_{ij}x_{i}\right)$\end{document}, where δij\begin{document}$\delta_{ij}$\end{document} is the Kronecker delta.

For a matrix X\begin{document}$X$\end{document}, X[i,:]\begin{document}$X\left[i,:\right]$\end{document} denotes the i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}-th row of X\begin{document}$X$\end{document}, and X[:,j]\begin{document}$X\left[:,j\right]$\end{document} denotes the j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}-th column of X\begin{document}$X$\end{document}.

sec.title: 2.1.2 Preparations
sec.p: Let l\begin{document}$l$\end{document} be the number of cells in each condition, m\begin{document}$m$\end{document} be the number of conditions, n\begin{document}$n$\end{document} be the original dimension of proteome, i.e., the number of protein species in the proteome data, and s′\begin{document}$s^{\prime}$\end{document} be the original dimension of Raman spectra after the application of the Savitzky-Golay filter. In our main data, l=38,m=15,n=2058\begin{document}$l = 38, m = 15, n = 2058$\end{document}, and s′=599\begin{document}$s^{\prime}= 599$\end{document}.

Original preprocessed Raman data: Let(2.1)x^j′(i)=(xj1′(i)⋯xjs′′(i))\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin {array}{ll}\hat {\boldsymbol{x}}^{\prime (i)}_{j}=\left(x^{\prime (i)}_{j1}\,\cdots\, x^{\prime(i)}_{js^\prime}\right)\end {array}$$\end{document}

be a preprocessed Raman spectrum from cell j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} under condition i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} (see ‘Raman measurements and preprocessing of spectra’ in Materials and methods). The prime ′ denotes that the variable is the original preprocessed data. The x^j′(i)\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{j}^{\prime(i)}$\end{document}(1≤j≤l)\begin{document}$(1 \le j \le l)$\end{document} are collected in an l×s′\begin{document}$l \times s^{\prime}$\end{document} matrix:(2.2)Xi′=[x^1′(i)⋮x^l′(i)]=(xjk′(i))1≤j≤l,1≤k≤s′.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle X_{i}^{\prime}=\left[\begin{matrix}\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{1}^{\prime(i)}\\\vdots \\\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{l}^{\prime(i)}\end{matrix}\right]=\left(x_{j k}^{\prime(i)}\right)_{1 \leq j \leq l, 1 \leq k \leq s^{\prime}}.$$\end{document}

Combining Xi′\begin{document}$X_{i}^{\prime}$\end{document} from different conditions, one can define an (lm)×s′\begin{document}$(lm) \times s^{\prime}$\end{document} matrix(2.3)X′=[X1′⋮Xm′],\begin{document}$$\displaystyle X^{\prime}=\left[\begin{matrix}X_{1}^{\prime}\\\vdots \\X_{m}^{\prime}\end{matrix}\right],$$\end{document}
which contains all the preprocessed Raman data.

PCA and centering: Before LDA, PCA was first applied to the preprocessed Raman data to reduce noise. The covariance matrix is(2.4)CX′:=1lm−1(X′−1lm1lm(1lm)⊤X′)⊤(X′−1lm1lm(1lm)⊤X′),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle C_{X^{\prime}} := \frac{1}{lm-1}\left(X^{\prime}- \frac{1}{lm}\boldsymbol{1}_{lm}(\boldsymbol{1}_{lm})^{\top}X^{\prime}\right)^{\top}\left(X^{\prime}- \frac{1}{lm}\boldsymbol{1}_{lm}(\boldsymbol{1}_{lm})^{\top}X^{\prime}\right),$$\end{document}

which is positive semi-definite. PCA is formulated as the following eigenvalue problem of CX′\begin{document}$C_{X^{\prime}}$\end{document}:(2.5)CX′VPCA=VPCAΛPCA,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle C_{X^{\prime}}V_{\mathrm{PCA}}= V_{\mathrm{PCA}}\Lambda_{\mathrm{PCA}},$$\end{document}

where ΛPCA\begin{document}$\Lambda_{\mathrm{PCA}}$\end{document} is a diagonal matrix with the eigenvalues of CX′\begin{document}$C_{X^{\prime}}$\end{document} as its diagonal elements in decreasing order from the upper left, and VPCA\begin{document}$V_{\mathrm{PCA}}$\end{document} is an orthogonal matrix consisting of the eigenvectors of CX′\begin{document}$C_{X^{\prime}}$\end{document} as its columns. Using the first s (1≤s≤s′)\begin{document}$s\ (1 \le s \le s^{\prime})$\end{document} columns of VPCA =[w1⋯ws′]\begin{document}$ V_{\text {PCA }}=\left[\begin{matrix}\boldsymbol{w}_{1} & \cdots & \boldsymbol{w}_{s^{\prime}}\end{matrix}\right]$\end{document}, i.e., the columns corresponding to the first s\begin{document}$s$\end{document} largest eigenvalues, we obtain an (lm)×s\begin{document}$(lm) \times s$\end{document} matrix representing the post PCA data:(2.6)X:=(X′−1lm1lm(1lm)⊤X′)[w1⋯ws]\begin{document}$$\displaystyle X:=\left(X^{\prime}-\frac{1}{l m} \mathbf{1}_{l m}\left(\mathbf{1}_{l m}\right)^{\top} X^{\prime}\right)\left[\begin{matrix}\boldsymbol{w}_{1} & \cdots & \boldsymbol{w}_{s} \end{matrix}\right]$$\end{document}

Here, wk\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{w}_{k}$\end{document} is the k\begin{document}$k$\end{document}-th PCA coefficient vector, and s\begin{document}$s$\end{document} is the reduced dimension of the Raman spectra. The subtraction of the 1lm1lm(1lm)⊤X′\begin{document}$\frac{1}{lm}\boldsymbol{1}_{lm}(\boldsymbol{1}_{lm})^{\top}X^{\prime}$\end{document} is for centering the data. In our case, the top 218 principal components (i.e. s=218\begin{document}$s = 218$\end{document}) explaining 98% of the variance were used to reduce noise and dimensionality.

Let x^j(i)\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{j}^{(i)}$\end{document} be the post PCA Raman spectrum from cell j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} under condition i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}, i.e.,(2.7)x^j(i)=(xj1(i)⋯xjs(i)),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin {array}{ll}\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{j}^{(i)}=\left(\begin{matrix}x_{j 1}^{(i)} & \cdots & x_{j s}^{(i)} \end{matrix}\right)\end {array},$$\end{document}

and Xi\begin{document}$X_{i}$\end{document} be the collection of x^j(i)\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{j}^{(i)}$\end{document} (1≤j≤l)\begin{document}$(1 \le j \le l)$\end{document}, i.e.,(2.8)Xi=[x^1(i)⋮x^l(i)]=(xjk(i))1≤j≤l,1≤k≤s\begin{document}$$\displaystyle X_{i}=\left[\begin{matrix}\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{1}^{(i)}\\\vdots \\\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{l}^{(i)}\end{matrix}\right]=\left(x_{j k}^{(i)}\right)_{1 \leq j \leq l, 1 \leq k \leq s}$$\end{document}
Then, X\begin{document}$X$\end{document} is written as(2.9)X=[X1⋮Xm]\begin{document}$$\displaystyle X=\left[\begin{matrix}X_{1} \\\vdots \\X_{m}\end{matrix}\right]$$\end{document}
From Equation 2.6,(2.10)(1lm)⊤X=0,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left(\boldsymbol{1}_{lm}\right)^{\top}X = \boldsymbol{0},$$\end{document}
namely, for any k\begin{document}$k$\end{document},(2.11)∑i=1m∑j=1lxjk(i)=0,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{l}x_{jk}^{(i)}= 0,$$\end{document}
which means that the post PCA data is centered.

Population average in each condition: Let x^i\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{i}$\end{document} be the population average of the post PCA spectra of cells under condition i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}. Then,(2.12)x^i=1l(1l)⊤Xi.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{equation*}\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{i}= \frac{1}{l}\left(\boldsymbol{1}_{l}\right)^{\top}X_{i}.\end{equation*}$$\end{document}

Also,(2.13)x^i=(x¯i1⋯x¯is)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll}\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{i}= \left(\begin{matrix} \bar{x}_{i 1} &\cdots & \bar{x}_{i s}\end{matrix}\right) \end{array}$$\end{document}
where(2.14)x‾ik=1l∑j=1lxjk(i).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{equation*}\bar{x}_{ik}= \frac{1}{l}\sum_{j=1}^{l}x_{jk}^{(i)}.\end{equation*}$$\end{document}

We define an m×s\begin{document}$m \times s$\end{document} matrix(2.15)X¯=[x^1⋮x^m]=(x¯ik)1≤i≤m,1≤k≤s.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll} \bar{X}=\left[\begin{matrix}\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{1} \\\vdots \\\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{m}\end{matrix}\right]=\left(\bar{x}_{i k}\right)_{1 \leq i \leq m, 1 \leq k \leq s}\end{array}.$$\end{document}

Each row of X‾\begin{document}$\bar{X}$\end{document} corresponds to a condition. From Equation 2.11 and Equation 2.14, for any k\begin{document}$k$\end{document},(2.16)∑i=1mx¯ik=0.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{m}\bar{x}_{ik}= 0.$$\end{document}
Namely,(2.17)(1m)⊤X¯=0.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left(\boldsymbol{1}_{m}\right)^{\top}\bar{X}= \boldsymbol{0}.$$\end{document}
These relations mean that X‾\begin{document}$\bar{X}$\end{document} is also centered.

LDA: The within-class covariance matrix is(2.18)CI:=1m∑i=1m1l−1(Xi−1l1l(1l)⊤Xi)⊤(Xi−1l1l(1l)⊤Xi)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle C_{I}:=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\frac{1}{l-1}\left(X_{i}- \frac{1}{l}\boldsymbol{1}_{l}\left(\boldsymbol{1}_{l}\right)^{\top}X_{i}\right)^{\top}\left(X_{i}- \frac{1}{l}\boldsymbol{1}_{l}\left(\boldsymbol{1}_{l}\right)^{\top}X_{i}\right)$$\end{document}(2.19)=1m∑i=1m1l−1(Xi−1lx^i)⊤(Xi−1lx^i).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\frac{1}{l-1}\left(X_{i}- \boldsymbol{1}_{l}\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{i}\right)^{\top}\left(X_{i}- \boldsymbol{1}_{l}\hat{\boldsymbol{x}}_{i}\right).$$\end{document}

Here, Equation 2.12 was used. The between-class covariance matrix is(2.20)CB:=1m−1(X¯−1m1m(1m)⊤X¯)⊤(X¯−1m1m(1m)⊤X¯)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle C_{B}:=\frac{1}{m-1}\left(\bar{X}- \frac{1}{m}\boldsymbol{1}_{m}\left(\boldsymbol{1}_{m}\right)^{\top}\bar{X}\right)^{\top}\left(\bar{X}- \frac{1}{m}\boldsymbol{1}_{m}\left(\boldsymbol{1}_{m}\right)^{\top}\bar{X}\right)$$\end{document}(2.21)=1m−1X¯⊤X¯.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\frac{1}{m-1}\bar{X}^{\top}\bar{X}.$$\end{document}

Here, Equation 2.17 was used. Assume rank(CI)=s\begin{document}$\mathrm{rank}(C_{I}) = s$\end{document} and rank(CB)=m−1\begin{document}$\mathrm{rank}(C_{B}) = m-1$\end{document} (the maximum possible values). In fact, rank(CI)=s\begin{document}$\mathrm{rank}(C_{I}) = s$\end{document} and rank(CB)=m−1\begin{document}$\mathrm{rank}(C_{B}) = m-1$\end{document} in our data. Note that s>m−1\begin{document}$s \gt m-1$\end{document}. From the definitions of CI\begin{document}$C_{I}$\end{document} and CB\begin{document}$C_{B}$\end{document} above, both are positive semi-definite. LDA is formulated as the following generalized eigenvalue problem:(2.22)CBVLDA=CIVLDAΛLDA′,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle C_{B}V_{\mathrm{LDA}}= C_{I}V_{\mathrm{LDA}}\Lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}}^{\prime},$$\end{document}

where ΛLDA′\begin{document}$\Lambda^{\prime}_{\mathrm{LDA}}$\end{document} is a diagonal matrix, and VLDA\begin{document}$V_{\mathrm{LDA}}$\end{document} is an s×(m−1)\begin{document}$s \times (m-1)$\end{document} matrix that simultaneously diagonalizes CB\begin{document}$C_{B}$\end{document} and CI\begin{document}$C_{I}$\end{document} (to ΛLDA\begin{document}$\Lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}}$\end{document}):(2.23)(VLDA)⊤CBVLDA=(VLDA)⊤CIVLDAΛLDA′=ΛLDA.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left(V_{\mathrm{LDA}}\right)^{\top}C_{B}V_{\mathrm{LDA}}= \left(V_{\mathrm{LDA}}\right)^{\top}C_{I}V_{\mathrm{LDA}}\Lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}}^{\prime}= \Lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}}.$$\end{document}

Here, the diagonal elements in ΛLDA′\begin{document}$\Lambda^{\prime}_{\mathrm{LDA}}$\end{document} were in decreasing order from the upper left. In our analysis, the columns of VLDA\begin{document}$V_{\mathrm{LDA}}$\end{document} were normalized.

Using VLDA\begin{document}$V_{\mathrm{LDA}}$\end{document}, we obtain an m×(m−1)\begin{document}$m \times (m-1)$\end{document} matrix representing the post LDA data(2.24)R:=X¯VLDA.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle R := \bar{X}V_{\mathrm{LDA}}.$$\end{document}

Each row of R\begin{document}$R$\end{document} represents a dimension-reduced Raman spectrum of each condition. Let us write the h\begin{document}$h$\end{document}-th (1≤h≤m−1)\begin{document}$\left(1 \le h \le m-1\right) $\end{document} column of R\begin{document}$R$\end{document} as(2.25)rh:=(r1h⋮rmh).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \boldsymbol{r}_{h}:=\left(\begin{matrix}r_{1 h} \\\vdots \\r_{m h}\end{matrix}\right) .$$\end{document}

Then,(2.26)R=[r1⋯rm−1]=(rih)1≤i≤m,1≤h≤m−1.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle R=\left[\begin{matrix}\boldsymbol{r}_{1} & \cdots & \boldsymbol{r}_{m-1} \end{matrix}\right]=\left(r_{i h}\right)_{1 \leq i \leq m, 1 \leq h \leq m-1} .$$\end{document}

Transforming R⊤R\begin{document}$R^{\top}R$\end{document} gives(2.27)R⊤R=(VLDA)⊤X¯⊤X¯VLDA\begin{document}$$\displaystyle R^{\top}R =\left(V_{\mathrm{LDA}}\right)^{\top}\bar{X}^{\top}\bar{X}V_{\mathrm{LDA}}$$\end{document}(2.28)=(m−1)(VLDA)⊤CBVLDA(∵2.20)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \qquad= (m-1)\left(V_{\mathrm{LDA}}\right)^{\top}C_{B}V_{\mathrm{LDA}}\quad (\because 2.20)$$\end{document}(2.29)=(m−1)ΛLDA(∵2.23).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =(m-1)\Lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}}\quad (\because {2.23}).$$\end{document}

Therefore, R⊤R\begin{document}$R^{\top}R$\end{document} is a diagonal matrix, and rh\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{r}_{h}$\end{document} are orthogonal to each other. As all the diagonal elements of the diagonal matrix ΛLDA\begin{document}$\Lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}}$\end{document} is positive, rank(R)=m−1\begin{document}$\mathrm{rank}(R) = m-1$\end{document}. Furthermore,(2.30)(1m)⊤R=(1m)⊤X¯VLDA\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left(\boldsymbol{1}_{m}\right)^{\top}R=\left(\boldsymbol{1}_{m}\right)^{\top}\bar{X}V_{\mathrm{LDA}}$$\end{document}(2.31)=(0m−1)⊤(∵2.17).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left(\boldsymbol{0}_{m-1}\right)^{\top}\quad (\because {2.17}).$$\end{document}
Namely, for any h\begin{document}$h$\end{document},(2.32)∑i=1mrih=0.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{m} r_{ih}= 0.$$\end{document}
This means that, as the data is centered, all the columns of R\begin{document}$R$\end{document} are perpendicular to 1m\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{1}_{m}$\end{document}.

sec.title: Proteome
sec.p: Let(2.33)pj=(p1j⋮pmj)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \boldsymbol{p}_{j}=\left(\begin{matrix}p_{1 j}\\\vdots \\p_{m j}\end{matrix}\right)$$\end{document}

be the absolute abundances of the j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}-th protein. pj\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{j}$\end{document} are collected in an m×n\begin{document}$m \times n$\end{document} matrix:(2.34)P:=[p1⋯pn]=(pij)1≤i≤m,1≤j≤n.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle P:=\left[\begin{matrix}\boldsymbol{p}_{1} & \cdots & \boldsymbol{p}_{n}\end{matrix}\right]=\left(p_{i j}\right)_{1 \leq i \leq m, 1 \leq j \leq n} .$$\end{document}
pij\begin{document}$p_{ij}$\end{document} is the absolute abundance of the j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}-th protein in condition i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}.

We assume rank(P)=m\begin{document}$\mathrm{rank}(P) = m$\end{document}, i.e., proteome vectors for different conditions are linearly independent. Actually, rank(P)=m\begin{document}$\mathrm{rank}(P) = m$\end{document} in our data. We also assume that proteins with zero expression in all the m\begin{document}$m$\end{document} conditions had been excluded from the proteome data.

sec.title: 2.1.3 Linear transformation between LDA Raman and proteome
sec.p: We define an m×m\begin{document}$m \times m$\end{document} matrix(2.35)RE:=[1mR]=[1mr1⋯rm−1]\begin{document}$$\displaystyle R_{E}:=\left[\begin{matrix}\mathbf{1}_{m} & R\end{matrix}\right]=\left[\begin{matrix}\mathbf{1}_{m} & \boldsymbol{r}_{1} & \cdots & \boldsymbol{r}_{m-1} \end{matrix}\right]$$\end{document}

We denote the first column of RE\begin{document}$R_{E}$\end{document} as r0\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{r}_{0}$\end{document}. Hence,(2.36)r0=1m.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \boldsymbol{r}_{0}= \boldsymbol{1}_{m}.$$\end{document}

From Equation 2.29 and Equation 2.31,(2.37)(RE)⊤RE=[(1m)⊤R⊤][1mR]=[m00R⊤R]=[m00(m−1)ΛLDA].\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{equation*}\left(R_{E}\right)^{\top}R_{E}= \begin{bmatrix}(\boldsymbol{1}_{m})^{\top}\\ R^{\top}\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix}\boldsymbol{1}_{m}&R\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}m&\boldsymbol{0}\\ \boldsymbol{0}&R^{\top}R\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}m&\boldsymbol{0}\\ \boldsymbol{0}&(m-1)\Lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}}\end{bmatrix}.\end{equation*}$$\end{document}

Therefore, (RE)⊤RE\begin{document}$\left(R_{E}\right)^{\top}R_{E}$\end{document} is also a diagonal matrix, and RE\begin{document}$R_{E}$\end{document} has full rank. For convenience, we write(2.38)(RE)⊤RE=ΛRE=(m(m−1)λLDA1⋱(m−1)λLDA(m−1)).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left(R_{E}\right)^{\top} R_{E}=\Lambda_{R_{E}}=\left(\begin{matrix}m & & & \\& (m-1) \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA} 1} & & \\& & \ddots & \\& & & (m-1) \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}(m-1)}\end{matrix}\right) .$$\end{document}

Here, we consider singular value decomposition (SVD) of RE\begin{document}$R_{E}$\end{document}; i.e.;(2.39)RE=UREΣRE(VRE)⊤,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle R_{E}= U_{R_{E}}\Sigma_{R_{E}}\left(V_{R_{E}}\right)^{\top},$$\end{document}

where ΣRE\begin{document}$\Sigma_{R_{E}}$\end{document} is a diagonal matrix whose diagonal elements are the singular values of RE\begin{document}$R_{E}$\end{document}, and (URE)⊤URE=(VRE)⊤VRE=I\begin{document}$\left(U_{R_{E}}\right)^{\top}U_{R_{E}}= \left(V_{R_{E}}\right)^{\top}V_{R_{E}}= I$\end{document}. Note that we can set VRE=I\begin{document}$V_{R_E}=I$\end{document} in the following way. Let(2.40)URE:=RE(ΛRE)−1/2.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle U_{R_E}:=R_E(\Lambda _{R_E})^{-1/2}.$$\end{document}

Then,(2.41)(URE)⊤URE=I,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle (U_{R_E})^\top U_{R_E}=I,$$\end{document}(2.42)(URE)⊤RE=(ΛRE)1/2.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle (U_{R_E})^\top R_E= (\Lambda_{R_E})^{{1}/{2}}.$$\end{document}
Thus, SVD of RE\begin{document}$R_E$\end{document} can be written as(2.43)RE=URE(ΛRE)1/2I.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle R_E=U_{R_E}(\Lambda_{R_E})^{{1}/{2}}I.$$\end{document}

As ΛRE\begin{document}$\Lambda_{R_E}$\end{document} is the eigenvalue matrix of (RE)⊤RE\begin{document}$(R_E)^\top R_E$\end{document},(2.44)(RE)⊤RE=ΛRE=(ΣRE)2\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left(R_E\right)^\top R_E=\Lambda_{R_E} =\left(\Sigma_{R_E}\right)^2$$\end{document}

and(2.45)ΣRE=(m(m−1)λLDA1⋱(m−1)λLDA(m−1)).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \Sigma_{R_{E}}=\left(\begin{matrix}\sqrt{m} & & & \\& \sqrt{(m-1) \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA} 1}} & & \\& & \ddots & \\& & & \sqrt{(m-1) \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}(m-1)}}\end{matrix}\right).$$\end{document}

Now, we consider linear transformation between P\begin{document}$P$\end{document} and RE\begin{document}$R_{E}$\end{document}. We introduce the n×m\begin{document}$n \times m$\end{document} coefficient matrix BE=[b0⋯bm−1]\begin{document}$B_{E}= \left[\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\cdots\boldsymbol{b}_{m-1}\right]$\end{document} that connects P\begin{document}$P$\end{document} and RE\begin{document}$R_{E}$\end{document} as(2.46)P=RE(BE)⊤\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll}P & =R_{E}\left(B_{E}\right)^{\top}\end{array}$$\end{document}(2.47)=[r0r1⋯rm−1][(b0)⊤(b1)⊤⋮(bm−1)⊤]\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left[\begin{matrix} \boldsymbol{r}_{0} \quad \boldsymbol{r}_{1} & \cdots & \boldsymbol{r}_{m-1}\end{matrix}\right] \left[\begin{matrix}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right)^{\top} \\\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{1}\right)^{\top} \\\vdots \\\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{m-1}\right)^{\top}\end{matrix}\right] $$\end{document}(2.48)=1m(b0)⊤+r1(b1)⊤+⋯+rm−1(bm−1)⊤.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\mathbf{1}_{m}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right)^{\top}+\boldsymbol{r}_{1}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{1}\right)^{\top}+\cdots+\boldsymbol{r}_{m-1}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{m-1}\right)^{\top}.$$\end{document}

RE\begin{document}$R_{E}$\end{document} has full rank and is therefore invertible. Thus, BE\begin{document}$B_{E}$\end{document} is obtained by(2.49)BE:=P⊤((RE)−1)⊤.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle B_{E}:= P^{\top}\left(\left(R_{E}\right)^{-1}\right)^{\top}.$$\end{document}

From the viewpoint of linear regression, b0\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{b}_{0}$\end{document} can be regarded as the constant terms and bh (1≤h≤m−1)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol {b}_h\ (1 \le h \le m-1)$\end{document} is the coefficients for the h\begin{document}$h$\end{document}-th LDA dimension.

We can rewrite P\begin{document}$P$\end{document} using the row vectors in RE\begin{document}$R_{E}$\end{document} and BE\begin{document}$B_{E}$\end{document}. Writing the i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}-th (1≤i≤m)\begin{document}$(1 \le i \le m)$\end{document} row of RE\begin{document}$R_{E}$\end{document} as r^i=(1ri1⋯ri(m−1))\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{i}= \left(1\,r_{i1}\cdots r_{i(m-1)}\right)$\end{document},(2.50)RE=[r^1⋮r^m]\begin{document}$$\displaystyle R_E=\begin{array} {ll}\begin{bmatrix} \hat {\boldsymbol{r}}_1 \\ \vdots \\ \hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_m \end{bmatrix}\end {array}$$\end{document}

Likewise, writing the j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}-th (1≤j≤n)\begin{document}$\left( 1 \le j \le n\right)$\end{document} row of BE\begin{document}$B_{E}$\end{document} as b^j=(bj0⋯bj(m−1))\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_j = \left(b_{j0} \cdots b_{j(m-1)}\right)$\end{document},(2.51)BE=[b^1⋮b^n]\begin{document}$$\displaystyle B_{E}=\left[\begin{matrix}\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{1} \\\vdots \\\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{n}\end{matrix}\right]$$\end{document}

Then, Equation 2.46 can be written in another way:(2.52)P=RE(BE)⊤=[r^1⋮r^m][(b^1)⊤⋯(b^n)⊤]=(r^1(b^1)⊤⋯r^1(b^n)⊤⋮⋱⋮r^m(b^1)⊤⋯r^m(b^n)⊤)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll}P & =R_{E}\left(B_{E}\right)^{\top} \\& =\left[\begin{matrix}\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{1} \\\vdots \\\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{m}\end{matrix}\right]\left[\begin{matrix}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{1}\right)^{\top} & \cdots & \left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{n}\right)^{\top}\end{matrix}\right]=\left(\begin{matrix}\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{1}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{1}\right)^{\top} & \cdots & \hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{1}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{n}\right)^{\top} \\\vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{m}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{1}\right)^{\top} & \cdots & \hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{m}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{n}\right)^{\top}\end{matrix}\right)\end{array}$$\end{document}
The interpretation of each vector is summarized in Appendix 1—table 9.

sec.title: 2.1.4 Relation between LDA Raman and ΩB\begin{document}$\Omega_{\mathrm{B}}$\end{document}
sec.p: Here, we discuss the spatial correspondence between the Raman distribution in LDA space and the normalized Raman-proteome coefficient proteome structure (Figure 6B and C).

sec.title: Connecting LDA Raman and Raman-omics transformation coefficients
sec.p: Let us consider (RE)⊤RE(BE)⊤\begin{document}$\left(R_{E}\right)^{\top}R_{E}\left(B_{E}\right)^{\top}$\end{document} in two ways. In this first approach,(2.53)(RE)⊤(RE(BE)⊤)=(RE)⊤P\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left(R_{E}\right)^{\top}\left(R_{E}\left(B_{E}\right)^{\top}\right) =\left(R_{E}\right)^{\top} P$$\end{document}(2.54)=[(r^1)⊤⋯(r^m)⊤]⟮p11⋯p1n⋮⋱⋮pm1⋯pmn⟯\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left[\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_1\right)^\top \cdots \left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_m\right)^\top\right]\left\lgroup\begin{matrix} p_{11} & \cdots & p_{1n} \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ p_{m1} & \cdots & p_{mn} \end{matrix} \right\rgroup $$\end{document}(2.55)=[∑i=1mpi1(r^i)⊤⋯∑i=1mpin(r^i)⊤]\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left[\begin{matrix} \sum\limits_{i=1}^{m}{p_{i1}\left(\hat {\boldsymbol {r}}_i\right)^\top} & \cdots & \sum\limits_{i=1}^{m}{p_{in} \left(\hat {\boldsymbol {r}}_i\right)^\top}\end{matrix} \right] $$\end{document}

In the second approach,(2.56)((RE)⊤RE)(BE)⊤=(ΣRE)2(BE)⊤\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array} {ll}\left(\left(R_{E}\right)^{\top} R_{E}\right)\left(B_{E}\right)^{\top} \\ =\left(\Sigma_{R_{E}}\right)^{2}\left(B_{E}\right)^{\top} \end{array}$$\end{document}(2.57)=(m(m−1)λLDA1⋱(m−1)λLDA(m−1))[(b^1)⊤⋯(b^n)⊤].\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left(\begin{matrix}m & & \\& (m-1) \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA} 1} & \\& \quad\qquad\qquad\ddots & \\& & (m-1) \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}(m-1)}\end{matrix}\right)\left[\begin{matrix} \left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_1\right)^\top & \cdots & \left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_n\right)^\top\end{matrix} \right].$$\end{document}

Comparing the two calculations yields(2.58)(RE)⊤P=(ΣRE)2(BE)⊤.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{equation*}\left(R_{E}\right)^{\top}P = \left(\Sigma_{R_{E}}\right)^{2}\left(B_{E}\right)^{\top}.\end{equation*}$$\end{document}

This is equivalent to(2.59)∑i=1mpij(r^i)⊤=(m(m−1)λLDA1⋱(m−1)λLDA(m−1))(b^j)⊤\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i j}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{i}\right)^{\top}=\left(\begin{matrix}m & & & \\& (m-1) \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA} 1} & & \\& & \ddots & \\& & & (m-1) \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}(m-1)}\end{matrix}\right)\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{j}\right)^{\top}$$\end{document}
for any protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}.

sec.title: Normalization with constant terms
sec.p: Next, we consider the normalization of the matrices in Equation 2.58 with constant terms.

We first define the normalized coefficient matrix as(2.60)BEnorm :=(1b11b10⋯b1(m−1)b10⋮⋮⋱⋮1bn1bn0⋯bn(m−1)bn0)=diag⁡(b0)−1BE.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle B_{E}^{\text {norm }}:=\left(\begin{matrix}1 & \frac{b_{11}}{b_{10}} & \cdots & \frac{b_{1(m-1)}}{b_{10}} \\\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\1 & \frac{b_{n 1}}{b_{n 0}} & \cdots & \frac{b_{n(m-1)}}{b_{n 0}}\end{matrix}\right)=\operatorname{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right)^{-1} B_{E} .$$\end{document}

This is normalization of the coefficients by the constant terms. Furthermore, we normalize ΣRE\begin{document}$\Sigma_{R_{E}}$\end{document} as(2.61)ΣREnorm:=1(ΣRE)11ΣRE=1mΣRE\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \Sigma_{R_{E}}^{\mathrm{norm}}:=\frac{1}{\left(\Sigma_{R_{E}}\right)_{11}} \Sigma_{R_{E}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{m} } \Sigma_{R_{E}}$$\end{document}(2.62)=(1m−1mλLDA1⋱m−1mλLDA(m−1)).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left(\begin{matrix}1 & & & \\& \sqrt{\frac{m-1}{m} \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA} 1}} & & \\& & \ddots & \\& & & \sqrt{\frac{m-1}{m} \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}(m-1)}}\end{matrix}\right) . $$\end{document}

Thus, the right-hand side of Equation 2.58 is(2.63)(ΣRE)2(BE)⊤=m(ΣREnorm)2(BEnorm)⊤diag(b0).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{equation*}\left(\Sigma_{R_{E}}\right)^{2}\left(B_{E}\right)^{\top}= m\left(\Sigma_{R_{E}}^{\mathrm{norm}}\right)^{2}\left(B_{E}^{\mathrm{norm}}\right)^{\top}\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right).\end{equation*}$$\end{document}

Since the first row of (RE)⊤\begin{document}$\left(R_{E}\right)^{\top}$\end{document} is (1m)⊤\begin{document}$\left(\boldsymbol{1}_{m}\right)^{\top}$\end{document}, one can rewrite the left-hand side of Equation 2.58 as(2.64)(RE)⊤P=[∑i=1mpi1(r^i)⊤∑i=1mpi1⋯∑i=1mpin(r^i)⊤∑i=1mpin](∑i=1mpi1⋱∑i=1mpin)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll}\left(R_{E}\right)^{\top} P & =\left[\begin{matrix}\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i 1}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{i}\right)^{\top}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i 1}} & \cdots & \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i n}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{i}\right)^{\top}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i n}}\end{matrix}\right]\left(\begin{matrix}\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i 1} & & \\& \ddots & \\& & \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i n}\end{matrix}\right) \\& \end{array}$$\end{document}(2.65)=[∑i=1mpi1(r^i)⊤∑i=1mpi1⋯∑i=1mpin(r^i)⊤∑i=1mpin]diag⁡((1m)⊤P).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left[\begin{matrix}\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i 1}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{i}\right)^{\top}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i 1}} & \cdots & \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i n}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{i}\right)^{\top}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i n}}\end{matrix}\right] \operatorname{diag}\left(\left(\mathbf{1}_{m}\right)^{\top} P\right).$$\end{document}

Here,(2.66)(1m)⊤P=(1m)⊤RE(BE)⊤\begin{document}$$\displaystyle (\boldsymbol{1}_m)^\top P=(\boldsymbol{1}_m)^\top R_E(B_E)^\top$$\end{document}(2.67)=[m(0m−1)⊤](BE)⊤\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left[m \quad\left(\boldsymbol{0}_{m-1}\right)^\top\right](B_E)^\top $$\end{document}(2.68)=m(b0)⊤.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =m\,(\boldsymbol {b}_0)^\top.$$\end{document}

Therefore,(2.69)b0=1m((1m)⊤p1⋮(1m)⊤pn)=(1m∑i=1mpi1⋮1m∑i=1mpin)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \boldsymbol{b}_{0}=\frac{1}{m}\left(\begin{matrix}\left(\mathbf{1}_{m}\right)^{\top} \boldsymbol{p}_{1} \\\vdots \\\left(\mathbf{1}_{m}\right)^{\top} \boldsymbol{p}_{n}\end{matrix}\right)=\left(\begin{matrix}\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i 1} \\\vdots \\\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i n}\end{matrix}\right)$$\end{document}

This relation indicates that the constant term for each protein is its average abundance. Consequently,(2.70)(RE)⊤P=m[∑i=1mpi1(r^i)⊤∑i=1mpi1…∑i=1mpin(r^i)⊤∑i=1mpin]diag⁡(b0)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left(R_{E}\right)^{\top} P=m\left[\begin{matrix}\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i 1}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{i}\right)^{\top}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i 1}} & \ldots & \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i n}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{i}\right)^{\top}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i n}}\end{matrix}\right] \operatorname{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right)$$\end{document}

Therefore, we obtain(2.71)[∑i=1mpi1(r^i)⊤∑i=1mpi1…∑i=1mpin(r^i)⊤∑i=1mpin]=(ΣREnorm)2(BEnorm)⊤.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left[\begin{matrix}\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i 1}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{i}\right)^{\top}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i 1}} & \ldots & \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i n}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{i}\right)^{\top}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i n}} \end{matrix}\right]=\left(\Sigma_{R_{E}}^{\mathrm{norm}}\right)^{2}\left(B_{E}^{\mathrm{norm}}\right)^{\top} .$$\end{document}

Equivalently,(2.72)∑i=1mpij(r^i)⊤∑i=1mpij=(1m−1mλLDA1⋱m−1mλLDA(m−1))(1bj1bj0⋮bj(m−1)bj0)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i j}\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{i}\right)^{\top}}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i j}}=\left(\begin{matrix}1 & & & \\& \frac{m-1}{m} \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA} 1} & & \\& & \ddots & \\& & & \frac{m-1}{m} \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}(m-1)}\end{matrix}\right)\left(\begin{matrix}1 \\\frac{b_{j 1}}{b_{j 0}} \\\vdots \\\frac{b_{j(m-1)}}{b_{j 0}}\end{matrix}\right)$$\end{document}

for any protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}. This means that the normalized coefficients of each protein are mainly determined by the weighted averages of the Raman vectors, where the weights are the abundances of the protein.

As we already saw in Equation 7 or Equation 8 in Materials and methods, this equation also shows that protein pairs whose abundance ratio remains constant over all the conditions have identical normalized coefficients.

sec.title: Special case – condition-specific protein
sec.p: Consider an imaginary condition-specific protein γ\begin{document}$\gamma$\end{document} whose abundance is c (>0)\begin{document}$c\ (\gt 0)$\end{document} under condition Γ\begin{document}$\Gamma$\end{document} and zero under the other conditions, i.e.,(2.73)piγ={0(i≠Γ)c(i=Γ).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll}p_{i\gamma}= \begin{cases}0 & (i \neq \Gamma) \\ c & (i = \Gamma)\end{cases}. \end{array}$$\end{document}

From Equation 2.59,(2.74)(cc⋱c)(r^Γ)⊤=(m(m−1)λLDA1⋱(m−1)λLDA(m−1))(b^γ)⊤,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left(\begin{matrix}c & & & \\& c & & \\& & \ddots & \\& & & c\end{matrix}\right)\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{\Gamma}\right)^{\top}=\left(\begin{matrix}m & & & \\& (m-1) \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA} 1} & & \\& & \ddots & \\& & & (m-1) \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}(m-1)}\end{matrix}\right)\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{\gamma}\right)^{\top},$$\end{document}

which indicates that the LDA Raman of condition Γ\begin{document}$\Gamma$\end{document}, (r^Γ)⊤\begin{document}$\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{\Gamma}\right)^{\top}$\end{document} and Raman-proteome coefficients for Γ\begin{document}$\Gamma$\end{document}-specific protein γ\begin{document}$\gamma$\end{document}, (b^γ)⊤\begin{document}$\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{\gamma}\right)^{\top}$\end{document} are in the same orthant. The normalized version is obtained by dividing both sides by the first row (or from Equation 2.72):(2.75)(r^Γ)⊤=(1m−1mλLDA1⋱m−1mλLDA(m−1))(1bγ1bγ0⋮bγ(m−1)bγ0)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{\Gamma}\right)^{\top} =\left(\begin{matrix}1 & & & \\& \frac{m-1}{m} \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA} 1} & & \\& \quad\qquad\qquad \ddots & \\& & \frac{m-1}{m} \lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}(m-1)}\end{matrix}\right)\left(\begin{matrix}1 \\\frac{b_{\gamma 1}}{b_{\gamma 0}} \\\vdots \\\frac{b_{\gamma(m-1)}}{b_{\gamma 0}}\end{matrix}\right)$$\end{document}(2.76)=(ΣREnorm)2(b^γ)⊤bγ0,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left(\Sigma_{R_{E}}^{\mathrm{norm}}\right)^{2} \frac{\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{\gamma}\right)^{\top}}{b_{\gamma 0}},$$\end{document}

which shows that the LDA Raman of condition Γ\begin{document}$\Gamma$\end{document}, (r^Γ)⊤\begin{document}$\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{r}}_{\Gamma}\right)^{\top}$\end{document} and the normalized Raman-proteome coefficients of Γ\begin{document}$\Gamma$\end{document}-specific protein γ\begin{document}$\gamma$\end{document}, (b^γ)⊤/bγ0\begin{document}$\left.\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_{\gamma}\right)^{\top}\big/b_{\gamma0}\right.$\end{document} are in the same orthant.

sec.title: Application to main data
sec.p: The LDA Raman distribution shown in Figure 6B corresponds to ri^\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{r}_i}$\end{document} (scatterplots between different columns of RE\begin{document}$R_{E}$\end{document}). On the other hand, the normalized coefficient proteome structure in Figure 6C is the scatterplots between different columns of BEnorm\begin{document}$B_{E}^{\mathrm{norm}}$\end{document}. The above linear algebra explains the correspondence between the two. In addition, from Equation 2.72, one can understand that the homeostatic core distributes around the center of the structure because its member proteins are expressed in all the conditions.

Equation 2.76 was obtained by considering an imaginary protein whose expression levels were zero under all conditions except for one condition. To confirm this relation with actual data, we picked an almost-condition-specific protein (PaaE, highly expressed in LB condition) and a non-condition-specific protein (AcrR), and confirmed that the former approximately satisfied Equation 2.76, while the relation did not hold for the latter (Appendix 1—figure 10).

sec.title: 2.1.5 Relation between ΩB\begin{document}$\Omega_{\mathrm{B}}$\end{document} and ΩLE\begin{document}$\Omega_{\mathrm{LE}}$\end{document}
sec.p: Here, we discuss the spatial correspondence between the normalized Raman-proteome coefficient proteome structure and the csLE proteome structure (Figure 6C and D).

sec.title: csLE proteome structure
sec.p: Consider an undirected graph where each node corresponds to one type of protein. As previously explained, let the graph be a complete graph, namely every pair of nodes is connected by an edge. Each edge is weighted with cosine similarity between the two types of protein connected by the edge. Cosine similarity of protein i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} and protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} is given by(2.77)cos⁡θpipj:=pi⋅pj‖pi‖2‖pj‖2,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}:= \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\cdot\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}{\|\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\|_{2}\|\boldsymbol{p}_{j}\|_{2}},$$\end{document}

where pi⋅pj(=(pi)⊤pj)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\cdot\boldsymbol{p}_{j}\left(= \left(\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\right)^{\top}\boldsymbol{p}_{j}\right)$\end{document} is the inner product of pi\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{i}$\end{document} and pj\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{j}$\end{document}, and ‖pi‖2(=(pi)⊤pi)\begin{document}$\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}}\right\| _{2}\left(=\sqrt{\left(\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\right)^{\top}\boldsymbol{p}_{i}}\right)$\end{document} is the L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document}-norm (Euclidean norm) of pi\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{i}$\end{document}. Cosine similarity cos⁡θpipj\begin{document}$\cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}$\end{document} evaluates how similar the expression patterns are between protein i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} and protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}. When the abundance ratio between protein i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} and protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} remains constant over all the m\begin{document}$m$\end{document} conditions, cos⁡θpipj\begin{document}$\cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}$\end{document} takes the maximum value 1.

The adjacency matrix of this graph is given by(2.78)A:=(cos⁡θpipj)1≤i,j≤n=(pi⋅pj‖pi‖2‖pj‖2)1≤i,j≤n\begin{document}$$\displaystyle A := \left( \cos \theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i} \boldsymbol{p}_{j}} \right)_{1\leq i,j \leq n} = \left( \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_{i} \cdot \boldsymbol{p}_{j}}{\| \boldsymbol{p}_{i} \|_{2} \| \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \|_{2}}\right)_{1\leq i,j \leq n} $$\end{document}
and the degree matrix of this graph is(2.79)D=diag(A1n).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle D =\mathrm{diag}\left(A\boldsymbol{1}_{n}\right).$$\end{document}
