For simplicity, diagonal element (i,i)\begin{document}$(i,i)$\end{document} of A\begin{document}$A$\end{document} is cos⁡θpipi=1\begin{document}$\cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\boldsymbol{p}_{i}}= 1$\end{document} for any protein i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}, i.e., each node has a loop. A\begin{document}$A$\end{document} is n×n\begin{document}$n \times n$\end{document} and real symmetric and D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} is n×n\begin{document}$n \times n$\end{document} and diagonal. Then, the Laplacian matrix is given by(2.80)L:=D−A,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle L := D - A,$$\end{document}

which is an n×n\begin{document}$n \times n$\end{document} symmetric matrix, and the symmetric normalized Laplacian is given by(2.81)Lsym=D−1/2LD−1/2=I−D−1/2AD−1/2,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle L_{\mathrm{sym}}= D^{-1/2}LD^{-1/2}= I - D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2},$$\end{document}
which is an n×n\begin{document}$n \times n$\end{document} symmetric matrix.

Here, we define P^\begin{document}$\hat{P}$\end{document} by normalizing the columns of P=[p1⋯pn]\begin{document}$P = \left[\begin{matrix}\boldsymbol{p}_{1} &\cdots &\boldsymbol{p}_{n}\end{matrix}\right]$\end{document}:(2.82)P^:=[p1‖p1‖2⋯pn‖pn‖2]=Pdiag⁡(P⊤P)−1/2\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll}\hat{P}:=\left[\begin{matrix}\frac{\boldsymbol{p}_{1}}{\left\|\boldsymbol{p}_{1}\right\|_{2}} & \cdots & \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_{n}}{\left\|\boldsymbol{p}_{n}\right\|_{2}} \end{matrix}\right]=P \operatorname{diag}\left(P^{\top} P\right)^{-1 / 2}\end {array}$$\end{document}

By using P^\begin{document}$\hat{P}$\end{document}, A\begin{document}$A$\end{document} is rewritten as(2.83)A=(cos⁡θpipj)1≤i,j≤n=(pi⋅pj‖pi‖2‖pj‖2)1≤i,j≤n\begin{document}$$\displaystyle A = \left( \cos \theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i} \boldsymbol{p}_{j}} \right)_{1\leq i,j \leq n} = \left( \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_{i} \cdot \boldsymbol{p}_{j}}{\| \boldsymbol{p}_{i} \|_{2} \| \boldsymbol{p}_{j} \|_{2}}\right)_{1\leq i,j \leq n}$$\end{document}(2.84)=(Pdiag(P⊤P)−1/2)⊤Pdiag(P⊤P)−1/2\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left(P\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{-1/2}\right)^{\top}P\,\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{-1/2}$$\end{document}(2.85)=diag(P⊤P)−1/2P⊤Pdiag(P⊤P)−1/2\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{-1/2}P^{\top}P\,\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{-1/2}$$\end{document}(2.86)=P^⊤P^.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\hat{P}^{\top}\hat{P}.$$\end{document}
Consider an eigenproblem(2.87)LsymVsym=VsymΛLE,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{equation*}L_{\mathrm{sym}}V_{\mathrm{sym}}= V_{\mathrm{sym}}\Lambda_{\mathrm{LE}},\end{equation*}$$\end{document}

where ΛLE\begin{document}$\Lambda_{\mathrm{LE}}$\end{document} is an n×n\begin{document}$n \times n$\end{document} diagonal matrix in which the eigenvalues of Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} are arranged in increasing order from the upper left, and columns of Vsym\begin{document}$V_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} are the normalized eigenvectors of Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} corresponding to the eigenvalues. Denote(2.88)ΛLE=(λLE0⋱λLE(n−1))\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \Lambda_{\mathrm{LE}}=\left(\begin{matrix}\lambda_{\mathrm{LE} 0} & & \\& \ddots & \\& & \lambda_{\mathrm{LE}(n-1)}\end{matrix}\right)$$\end{document}
Here, Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} has the following four characteristics:

list-item.p: Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} is positive semi-definite. See, for example, von Luxburg, 2007 for the proof.

In an undirected graph with non-negative weights, the number of separated graph components equals the multiplicity of the eigenvalue zero of Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document}. See, for example, von Luxburg, 2007 for the proof. Since our proteome graph is connected, our Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} has the single eigenvalue zero.

From Equation 2.86, rank(A)=rank(P^)\begin{document}${\rm rank}(A) = {\rm rank}(\hat{P})$\end{document}. Here, it is obvious that diag(P⊤P)−1/2\begin{document}$\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{-1/2}$\end{document} has full rank, hence, rank(P^)=rank(P)=m\begin{document}${\rm rank} (\hat{P}) = {\rm rank}(P) = m$\end{document}. Therefore, rank(A)=m\begin{document}${\rm rank}(A) = m$\end{document}. Obviously, D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} has full rank by definition and thus, rank(D−1/2AD−1/2)=rank(A)=m\begin{document}${\rm rank}\left(D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}\right) = {\rm rank}(A) = m$\end{document}. Therefore, D−1/2AD−1/2\begin{document}$D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}$\end{document} has n−m\begin{document}$n-m$\end{document} singular values of zero. Since D−1/2AD−1/2\begin{document}$D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}$\end{document} is symmetric, its singular values and eigenvalues are the same. Thus, D−1/2AD−1/2\begin{document}$D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}$\end{document} has n−m\begin{document}$n-m$\end{document} singular values of zero. Therefore, Lsym(=I−D−1/2AD−1/2)\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}\left(= I - D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}\right)$\end{document} has n−m\begin{document}$n-m$\end{document} singular values of 1(=1−0)\begin{document}$1 (= 1-0)$\end{document}.

For any n\begin{document}$n$\end{document}-dimensional vector x\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{x}$\end{document}, x⊤D−1/2AD−1/2x\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{x}^{\top}D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}\boldsymbol{x}$\end{document} =(P^D−1/2x)⊤P^D−1/2x\begin{document}$= \left( \hat{P}D^{-1/2}\boldsymbol{x} \right)^{\top}\hat{P}D^{-1/2}\boldsymbol{x}$\end{document} =‖P^D−1/2x‖22≥0\begin{document}$=\left\|\hat{P}D^{-1/2}\boldsymbol{x}\right\|_2^2 \geq 0$\end{document}. Therefore, D−1/2AD−1/2\begin{document}$D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}$\end{document} is positive semi-definite, and all of the eigenvalues of Lsym(=I−D−1/2AD−1/2)\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}\left(= I - D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}\right)$\end{document} are less than or equal to one.

sec.p: By these four points, we see that the eigenvalues of Lsym(=I−D−1/2AD−1/2)\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}\left(= I - D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}\right)$\end{document} satisfy(2.89)0=λLE0<λLE1≤⋯≤λLE(m−1)<λLEm=λLE(m+1)=⋯=λLE(n−1)=1.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle 0 = \lambda_{\mathrm{LE}0}\lt \lambda_{\mathrm{LE}1}\le \cdots \le \lambda_{\mathrm{LE}(m-1)}\lt \lambda_{\mathrm{LE}m}= \lambda_{\mathrm{LE}(m+1)}= \cdots = \lambda_{\mathrm{LE}(n-1)}= 1.$$\end{document}

Now we define an m×m\begin{document}$m\times m$\end{document} matrix Λ~LE\begin{document}$\tilde{\Lambda}_{\mathrm{LE}}$\end{document} as(2.90)Λ~LE=(0λLE1⋱λLE(m−1))\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll}\tilde{\Lambda}_{\mathrm{LE}}=\left(\begin{matrix}0 & & & \\& \lambda_{\mathrm{LE} 1} & & \\& & \ddots & \\& & & \lambda_{\mathrm{LE}(m-1)}\end{matrix}\right)\end{array}$$\end{document}
Then, we can write(2.91)ΛLE=(Λ~LEI)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \Lambda_{\mathrm{LE}}=\left(\begin{matrix}\tilde{\Lambda}_{\mathrm{LE}} & \\& \\& I\end{matrix}\right)$$\end{document}

Let V~sym\begin{document}$\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} be the first m\begin{document}$m$\end{document} columns of Vsym\begin{document}$V_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document}:(2.92)V~sym=[v^sym,1⋮v^sym,n]=[vsym,10⋯vsym,1(m−1)⋮⋱⋮vsym,n0⋯vsym,n(m−1)]\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}=\left[\begin{matrix}\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{sym}, 1} \\\vdots \\\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{sym}, n}\end{matrix}\right]=\left[\begin{matrix}v_{\mathrm{sym}, 10} & \cdots & v_{\mathrm{sym}, 1(m-1)} \\\vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\v_{\mathrm{sym}, n 0} & \cdots & v_{\mathrm{sym}, n(m-1)}\end{matrix}\right]\end{array}$$\end{document}

The truncated version of the eigenproblem is(2.93)LsymV~sym=V~symΛ~LE.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle L_{\mathrm{sym}}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}= \tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\tilde{\Lambda}_{\mathrm{LE}}.$$\end{document}

v^sym,i:=(vsym,i0⋯vsym,i(m−1))\begin{document}$\begin{array}{ll}\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\text {sym}, i}:=\left(\begin{matrix}v_{\text {sym}, i 0} & \cdots & v_{\text {sym}, i(m-1)}\end{matrix}\right)\end{array}$\end{document} is the i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}-th row of V~sym\begin{document}$\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} and provides a new m\begin{document}$m$\end{document}-dimensional representation of the i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}-th protein. This representation of the proteome reflects distance between each protein pair in terms of cosine similarity.

Here we clarify the correspondence of the eigenproblems defined by the normalized Laplacian matrices Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} and Lrw\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document}. Let(2.94)V~rw=D−1/2V~sym.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}= D^{-1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}.$$\end{document}

Then, this eigenproblem can also be regarded as the following generalized eigenproblem,(2.95)LV~rw=DV~rwΛ~LE.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle L\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}= D\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\tilde{\Lambda}_{\mathrm{LE}}.$$\end{document}

Remembering that Lrw=D−1L\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{rw}}= D^{-1}L$\end{document}, we can further transform it into an eigneproblem(2.96)LrwV~rw=V~rwΛ~LE.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle L_{\mathrm{rw}}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}= \tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\tilde{\Lambda}_{\mathrm{LE}}.$$\end{document}
This is the form of eigenproblem that we explained previously in ‘Global proteome structures based on stoichiometric balance’ in Materials and methods. In this section, we discuss it later.
The eigenproblem (Equation 2.87) can be transformed to(2.97)D−1/2AD−1/2Vsym=VsymM,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}V_{\mathrm{sym}}= V_{\mathrm{sym}}M,$$\end{document}
where(2.98)M:=I−ΛLE=(I−Λ~LEO).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle M:=I-\Lambda_{\mathrm{LE}}=\left(\begin{matrix}I-\tilde{\Lambda}_{\mathrm{LE}} & \\& O\end{matrix}\right) .$$\end{document}

This means that the columns of Vsym\begin{document}$V_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} are also the (normalized) eigenvectors of D−1/2AD−1/2\begin{document}$D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}$\end{document}, and M\begin{document}$M$\end{document} is the corresponding eigenvalue matrix.

Defining an m×m\begin{document}$m \times m$\end{document} matrix M~\begin{document}$\tilde{M}$\end{document} as(2.99)M~=I−Λ~LE=(11−λLE1⋱1−λLE(m−1)),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll}\tilde{M}=I-\tilde{\Lambda}_{\mathrm{LE}}=\left(\begin{matrix}1 & & & \\& 1-\lambda_{\mathrm{LE} 1} & & \\& & \ddots & \\& & & 1-\lambda_{\mathrm{LE}(m-1)}\end{matrix}\right)\end{array},$$\end{document}
we can write(2.100)M=(M~O).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle M=\left(\begin{matrix}\tilde{M} & \\& \\& O\end{matrix}\right) .$$\end{document}
Note that(2.101)1>1−λLE1≥⋯≥1−λLE(m−1)>0.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{equation*}1 \gt 1-\lambda_{\mathrm{LE}1}\ge \cdots \ge 1-\lambda_{\mathrm{LE}(m-1)}\gt 0.\end{equation*}$$\end{document}
Equation 2.97 is further transformed into(2.102)A=D1/2VsymM(Vsym)⊤D1/2.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle A = D^{1/2}V_{\mathrm{sym}}M\left(V_{\mathrm{sym}}\right)^{\top}D^{1/2}.$$\end{document}

Comparing Equation 2.102 and Equation 2.85 leads to(2.103)diag(P⊤P)−1/2P⊤Pdiag(P⊤P)−1/2=D1/2VsymM(Vsym)⊤D1/2.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{-1/2}P^{\top}P\,\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{-1/2}= D^{1/2}V_{\mathrm{sym}}M\left(V_{\mathrm{sym}}\right)^{\top}D^{1/2}.$$\end{document}

sec.title: Connecting Raman-proteome transformation coefficients and csLE proteome
sec.p: We consider P⊤P\begin{document}$P^{\top}P$\end{document} in two ways. First, from Equation 2.103,(2.104)P⊤P=diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2VsymM(Vsym)⊤D1/2diag(P⊤P)1/2=diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2VsymM1/2(diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2VsymM1/2)⊤=diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2(V~symM~1/2|O)(diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2(V~symM~1/2|O))⊤=(diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~symM~1/2|O)(diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~symM~1/2|O)⊤=(diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~symM~1/2|O)((diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~symM~1/2)⊤O)=diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~symM~1/2(diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~symM~1/2)⊤.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll}P^{\top}P=\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}V_{\mathrm{sym}}M\left(V_{\mathrm{sym}}\right)^{\top}D^{1/2}\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2} \\\qquad=\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}V_{\mathrm{sym}}M^{1/2}\left(\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}V_{\mathrm{sym}}M^{1/2}\right)^{\top} \\ \qquad=\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}\left(\begin{matrix}\left.\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\tilde{M}^{1/2} \big| O\right.\end{matrix} \right) \left(\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}\left(\begin{matrix}\left.\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\tilde{M}^{1/2} \big| O\right.\end{matrix} \right) \right)^{\top} \\ \qquad=\left(\begin{matrix}\left.\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2} D^{1/2} \tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\tilde{M}^{1/2} \big| O\right.\end{matrix} \right) \left(\begin{matrix}\left.\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2} D^{1/2} \tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\tilde{M}^{1/2} \big| O\right.\end{matrix} \right)^{\top} \\ \qquad=\left(\begin{matrix}\left.\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2} D^{1/2} \tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\tilde{M}^{1/2} \big| O\right.\end{matrix} \right) \left(\begin{matrix}\left(\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2} D^{1/2} \tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\tilde{M}^{1/2}\right)^{\top} \\ O\end{matrix} \right) \\ \qquad=\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\tilde{M}^{1/2}\left(\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\tilde{M}^{1/2}\right)^{\top}.\end{array}$$\end{document}

Since(2.105)D−1/2AD−1/2=D−1/2P^⊤P^D−1/2(∵(2.86))\begin{document}$$\displaystyle D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}=D^{-1/2}\hat{P}^{\top}\hat{P}D^{-1/2}\quad \left(\because (2.86)\right)$$\end{document}(2.106)=(P^D−1/2)⊤P^D−1/2,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left(\hat{P}D^{-1/2}\right)^{\top}\hat{P}D^{-1/2},$$\end{document}

the diagonal elements of M~\begin{document}$\tilde{M}$\end{document}, i.e., the positive eigenvalues of D−1/2AD−1/2\begin{document}$D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2}$\end{document} are the square of the singular values of P^D−1/2\begin{document}$\hat{P}D^{-1/2}$\end{document}. Compact SVD of P^D−1/2\begin{document}$\hat{P}D^{-1/2}$\end{document} is expressed as(2.107)P^D−1/2=ULEΣLE(VLE)⊤,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \hat{P}D^{-1/2}= U_{\mathrm{LE}}\Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}\left(V_{\mathrm{LE}}\right)^{\top},$$\end{document}

where ΣLE\begin{document}$\Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}$\end{document} is an m×m\begin{document}$m \times m$\end{document} diagonal matrix whose diagonal elements are the singular values in decreasing order from the upper left, and (ULE)⊤ULE=(VLE)⊤VLE=I\begin{document}$\left(U_{\mathrm{LE}}\right)^{\top}U_{\mathrm{LE}}= \left(V_{\mathrm{LE}}\right)^{\top}V_{\mathrm{LE}}= I$\end{document}. We then obtain(2.108)ΣLE=M~1/2=(11−λLE1⋱1−λLE(m−1))\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}=\tilde{M}^{1 / 2}=\left(\begin{matrix}1 & & & \\& \sqrt{1-\lambda_{\mathrm{LE} 1}} & & \\& & \ddots & \\& & & \sqrt{1-\lambda_{\mathrm{LE}(m-1)}}\end{matrix}\right)$$\end{document}

Thus,(2.109)P⊤P=diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~symΣLE(diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~symΣLE)⊤.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle P^{\top}P =\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}\left(\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}\right)^{\top}.$$\end{document}

On the other hand, from Equation 2.44 and Equation 2.46,(2.110)P⊤P=(RE(BE)⊤)⊤RE(BE)⊤=BE(RE)⊤RE(BE)⊤\begin{document}$$\displaystyle P^{\top}P = \left(R_{E}\left(B_{E}\right)^{\top}\right)^{\top}R_{E}\left(B_{E}\right)^{\top}=B_{E}\left(R_{E}\right)^{\top}R_{E}\left(B_{E}\right)^{\top}$$\end{document}(2.111)=BE(ΣRE)2BE⊤\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =B_{E}\left(\Sigma_{R_{E}}\right)^{2}B_{E}^{\top}$$\end{document}(2.112)=BEΣRE(BEΣRE)⊤.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =B_{E}\Sigma_{R_{E}}\left(B_{E}\Sigma_{R_{E}}\right)^{\top}.$$\end{document}

Therefore, comparing Equation 2.109 and Equation 2.112 yields(2.113)BEΣRE=diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~symΣLEΘ,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle B_{E}\Sigma_{R_{E}}=\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}\Theta,$$\end{document}

where Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} is an m×m\begin{document}$m \times m$\end{document} orthogonal matrix. We define the estimate of BE\begin{document}$B_{E}$\end{document} as(2.114)BEest:=diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~sym.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle B_{E}^{\mathrm{est}}:=\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}.$$\end{document}
By this notation, Equation 2.113 can be written as(2.115)BEΣRE=BEestΣLEΘ.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle B_{E}\Sigma_{R_{E}}= B_{E}^{\mathrm{est}}\Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}\Theta.$$\end{document}

Here, the left-hand side represents Raman-proteome linear transformation, whereas the right-hand side except for Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} is derived only from proteome data. Note that in order to derive Equation 2.113, LDA does not need to be applied to Raman data because P⊤P\begin{document}$P^{\top}P$\end{document} can be written in the form of BE(ΣRE)2(BE)⊤\begin{document}$B_{E}\left(\Sigma_{R_{E}}\right)^{2}\left(B_{E}\right)^{\top}$\end{document} even if LDA is not applied to Raman data.

sec.title: Normalization with constant terms
sec.p: Now we consider normalizing both sides of Equation 2.115 by the first columns.

With Equation 2.60 and Equation 2.61, the left-hand side of Equation 2.115 can be rewritten as(2.116)BEΣRE=mdiag(b0)BEnormΣREnorm.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle B_{E}\Sigma_{R_{E}}=\sqrt{m}\,\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right)B_{E}^{\mathrm{norm}}\Sigma_{R_{E}}^{\mathrm{norm}}.$$\end{document}

Likewise, for the right-hand side, the first column of BEest\begin{document}$B_{E}^{\mathrm{est}}$\end{document} (the estimated constant term) is(2.117)b0est:=BEest[:,1]=BEest(10⋮0)=diag⁡(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~sym(10⋮0).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}:=B_{E}^{\mathrm{est}}[:, 1]=B_{E}^{\mathrm{est}}\left(\begin{matrix}1 \\0 \\\vdots \\0\end{matrix}\right)=\operatorname{diag}\left(P^{\top} P\right)^{1 / 2} D^{1 / 2} \tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}\left(\begin{matrix}1 \\0 \\\vdots \\0\end{matrix}\right) .$$\end{document}

The first column of V~sym\begin{document}$\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} is the normalized eigenvector corresponding to the eigenvalue zero of Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document}.

By the definition of L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}, L1n=D1n−A1n=diag(D)−diag(D)=0n\begin{document}$L\boldsymbol{1}_n = D \boldsymbol{1}_n-A\boldsymbol{1}_n=\mathrm{diag}(D)-\mathrm{diag}(D)=\boldsymbol{0}_n$\end{document}. Hence, in general, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} has an eigenvalue zero and a corresponding eigenvector 1n\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{1}_n$\end{document}. Therefore, by the definition of Lrw\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document}, Lrw1n=D−1L1n=0n\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{rw}}\boldsymbol{1}_n = D^{-1}L \boldsymbol{1}_n = \boldsymbol{0}_n$\end{document}; Lrw\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document} also has an eigenvalue zero and a corresponding eigenvector 1n\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{1}_n$\end{document}. The eigenproblems LrwV~rw=V~rwΛ~LE\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{rw}}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}} = \tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}} \tilde{\Lambda}_{\mathrm{LE}}$\end{document} and LsymV~sym=V~symΛ~LE\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}} = \tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}} \tilde{\Lambda}_{\mathrm{LE}}$\end{document} are equivalent because one can obtain the eigenproblem of Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} by left multiplying both sides of the eigenproblem of Lrw\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document} by D1/2\begin{document}$D^{1/2}$\end{document} and that of Lrw\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document} by left multiplying both sides of the eigenproblem of Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} by D−1/2\begin{document}$D^{-1/2}$\end{document}. Eigenvalues of Lrw\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document} and Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} are identical, and V~rw=D−1/2V~sym\begin{document}$\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}=D^{-1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} holds for their eigenvectors. Therefore, Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} has an eigenvalue zero and a corresponding eigenvector D1/21n\begin{document}$D^{1/2}\boldsymbol{1}_n$\end{document}. The multiplicity of the eigenvalue zero of our Lsym\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document} is one, and its corresponding eigenvector is limited to D1/21n\begin{document}$D^{1/2}\boldsymbol{1}_n$\end{document}.

By writing D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} as(2.118)D=(d1⋱dn),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle D=\left(\begin{matrix}d_{1} & & \\& \ddots & \\& & d_{n}\end{matrix}\right),$$\end{document}(2.119)V~sym [:,1]=V~sym (10⋮0)=(∑i=1ndi)−1/2D1/21n.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \tilde{V}_{\text {sym }}[:, 1]=\tilde{V}_{\text {sym }}\left(\begin{matrix}1 \\0 \\\vdots \\0\end{matrix}\right)=\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{i}\right)^{-1 / 2} D^{1 / 2} \mathbf{1}_{n}.$$\end{document}

Thus, Equation 2.117 can be further transformed as(2.120)b0est=BEest[:,1]=(∑i=1ndi)−1/2diag⁡(P⊤P)1/2D1/2D1/21n=(∑i=1ndi)−1/2diag⁡(P⊤P)1/2D1n\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{array}{ll} \boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}=B_{E}^{\mathrm{est}}[:, 1] &=\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{i}\right)^{-1 / 2} \operatorname{diag}\left(P^{\top} P\right)^{1 / 2} D^{1 / 2} D^{1 / 2} \mathbf{1}_{n} \\& =\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{i}\right)^{-1 / 2} \operatorname{diag}\left(P^{\top} P\right)^{1 / 2} D \mathbf{1}_{n} \end{array}$$\end{document}(2.121)=1(∑i=1ndi)1/2(d1(p1)⊤p1⋮dn(pn)⊤pn).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\frac{1}{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{i}\right)^{1 / 2}}\left(\begin{matrix}d_{1} \sqrt{\left(\boldsymbol{p}_{1}\right)^{\top} \boldsymbol{p}_{1}} \\\vdots \\d_{n} \sqrt{\left(\boldsymbol{p}_{n}\right)^{\top} \boldsymbol{p}_{n}}\end{matrix}\right) .$$\end{document}

Remembering that both diag(P⊤P)\begin{document}$\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)$\end{document} and D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} are diagonal, we obtain(2.122)diag(b0est)=diag(BEest[:,1])=(∑i=1ndi)−1/2diag(P⊤P)1/2D.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}\right) =\mathrm{diag}\left(B_{E}^{\mathrm{est}}\left[:,1\right]\right) = \left({\sum_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}}\right)^{-1/2}\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D. $$\end{document}

Therefore, from Equation 2.114 and Equation 2.122, the ‘estimated coefficients’ normalized with the ‘estimated constants’ are(2.123)BEest,norm:=diag(b0est)−1BEest\begin{document}$$\displaystyle B_{E}^{\mathrm{est},\mathrm{norm}}:=\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}\right)^{-1}B_{E}^{\mathrm{est}}$$\end{document}(2.124)=diag(b0est)−1diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~sym\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}\right)^{-1}\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}$$\end{document}(2.125)=(∑i=1ndi)1/2D−1diag(P⊤P)−1/2diag(P⊤P)1/2D1/2V~sym\begin{document}$$\displaystyle = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}\right)^{1/2}D^{-1} \mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{-1/2} \mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D^{1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}$$\end{document}(2.126)=(∑i=1ndi)1/2D−1/2V~sym\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}\right)^{1/2}D^{-1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}$$\end{document}(2.127)=(∑i=1ndi)1/2V~rw.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}\right)^{1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}.$$\end{document}

We remark that the eigenproblem of Lrw\begin{document}$L_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document}, i.e.,(2.128)LrwV~rw=V~rwΛ~LE\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{equation*}L_{\mathrm{rw}}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}= \tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\tilde{\Lambda}_{\mathrm{LE}}\end{equation*}$$\end{document}

is equivalent to solving a minimization problem(2.129)arg min∑i,j‖v^rw,i−v^rw,j‖22cos⁡θpipjsubject to(V~rw)⊤DV~rw=I.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \mathrm {arg~min}\sum_{i,j}\left\|{\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},i} - \hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},j}}\right\| ^{2}_{2}\cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\quad \mathrm{subject\ to}\quad (\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}})^{\top}D\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}= I.$$\end{document}

Thus, the closer pi\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{i}$\end{document} and pj\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{j}$\end{document} are in terms of cosine similarity, the closer v^rw,i\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},i}$\end{document} and v^rw,j\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},j}$\end{document} (the i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}-th and j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}-th rows of V~rw\begin{document}$\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document}, respectively).

The relation between the minimization problem and the eigenproblem is the following. The objective function of the minimization problem is(2.130)∑i,j‖v^rw,i−v^rw,j‖22cos⁡θpipj=2∑idi‖v^rw,i‖22−2∑i,jAijv^rw,i⋅v^rw,j.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \sum_{i,j}\left\|{\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},i} - \hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},j}}\right\| ^{2}_{2}\cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}=2\sum_{i}d_{i}\left\|{\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},i}}\right\| ^{2}_{2} - 2\sum_{i,j}A_{ij}\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},i}\cdot\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},j}. $$\end{document}

Here,(2.131)tr((V~rw)⊤DV~rw)=∑idi‖v^rw,i‖22,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \mathrm{tr}\left(\left(\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right)^{\top}D\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right)=\sum_{i}d_{i}\left\|{\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},i}}\right\|^{2}_{2}, $$\end{document}(2.132)tr((V~rw)⊤AV~rw)=∑i,jAijv^rw,i⋅v^rw,j.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \mathrm{tr}\left(\left(\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right)^{\top}A\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right)=\sum_{i,j}A_{ij}\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},i}\cdot\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},j}.$$\end{document}

Therefore, Equation 2.130 can be transformed into(2.133)∑i,j‖v^rw,i−v^rw,j‖22cos⁡θpipj=2 tr((V~rw)⊤DV~rw)−2 tr((V~rw)⊤AV~rw)\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \sum_{i,j} \left\|{\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},i} - \hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw},j}}\right\|^{2}_{2}\cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i}\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}=2\ \mathrm{tr}\left(\left(\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right)^{\top}D\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right) - 2\ \mathrm{tr}\left(\left(\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right)^{\top}A\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right)$$\end{document}(2.134)=2 tr((V~rw)⊤LV~rw).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =2\ {\rm tr}\left(\left(\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right)^{\top}L\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right).$$\end{document}

Thus, the minimization problem is(2.135)arg mintr((V~rw)⊤LV~rw)subject to(V~rw)⊤DV~rw=I.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle {\rm arg~min}\quad {\rm tr}\left(\left(\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right)^{\top}L\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right) \quad \mathrm{subject\ to}\quad \left(\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right)^{\top}D\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}= I.$$\end{document}

This can be transformed into the generalized eigenproblem LV~rw=DV~rwΛ~LE\begin{document}$L\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}= D\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\tilde{\Lambda}_{\mathrm{LE}}$\end{document} by the method of Lagrange multipliers (Ghojogh et al., 2019).

Remember that this property of v^rw,i\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw}, i}$\end{document} and v^rw,j\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{v}}_{\mathrm{rw}, j}$\end{document} is analogous to that of b^inorm\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_i^{\mathrm{norm}}$\end{document} and b^jnorm\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}_j^{\mathrm{norm}}$\end{document} (the i\begin{document}$i$\end{document}-th and j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}-th rows of BEnorm\begin{document}$B_{E}^{\rm norm}$\end{document}, respectively) as explained in Section 2.1.4 in Appendix and ‘Raman-proteome correspondence matrix as a low-dimensional representation of proteome changes’ in Materials and methods.

By considering the first (upper-left) element of ΣLE\begin{document}$\Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}$\end{document} is one, the right side of Equation 2.113 can be transformed into(2.136)BEestΣLEΘ=diag(b0est)BEest,normΣLEΘ\begin{document}$$\displaystyle B_{E}^{\mathrm{est}}\Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}\Theta =\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}\right)B_{E}^{\mathrm{est},\mathrm{norm}}\Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}\Theta$$\end{document}(2.137)=[(∑i=1ndi)−1/2diag(P⊤P)1/2D][(∑i=1ndi)1/2V~rw]ΣLEΘ.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}\right)^{-1/2}\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D\right] \left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}\right)^{1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right] \Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}\Theta.$$\end{document}

Therefore, from Equations 2.115, 2.116, 2.136, 2.137,(2.138)mdiag⁡(b0)BEnormΣREnorm=diag⁡(b0est)BEest,normΣLEΘ\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \sqrt{m} \operatorname{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right) B_{E}^{\mathrm{norm}} \Sigma_{R_{E}}^{\mathrm{norm}} =\operatorname{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}\right) B_{E}^{\mathrm{est}, \mathrm{norm}} \Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}} \Theta$$\end{document}(2.139)=[(∑i=1ndi)−1/2diag⁡(P⊤P)1/2D][(∑i=1ndi)1/2V~rw]ΣLEΘ.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \quad=\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{i}\right)^{-1 / 2} \operatorname{diag}\left(P^{\top} P\right)^{1 / 2} D\right]\left[\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_{i}\right)^{1 / 2} \tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}\right] \Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}} \Theta .$$\end{document}
This is the equation that links the normalized Raman-proteome coefficient proteome structure and the csLE proteome structure.

sec.title: Mathematical interpretation of the obtained equation
sec.p: From Equation 2.139, if the distributions of BEnorm\begin{document}$B_{E}^{\mathrm{norm}}$\end{document} and V~rw\begin{document}$\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document} are similar, the diagonal matrix Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} must be similar to the identity matrix because large off-diagonal elements of Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} makes lower dimensions ‘mix’ much with the higher dimensions. In addition, the directions of diagonal matrices diag(b0)\begin{document}$\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right)$\end{document} and diag(P⊤P)1/2D\begin{document}$\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D$\end{document} must also be close to each other even if Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} is close to the identity matrix. Note that the first column of Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} also reflects the relation between b0\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{b}_{0}$\end{document} and b0est(∝diag(P⊤P)1/2D1n)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}\left(\propto \mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D\boldsymbol{1}_{n}\right)$\end{document}.
The obtained relation between Raman-proteome normalized coefficient structure and csLE structure is summarized in Appendix 1—table 10.

Note that the relation between ΣREnorm\begin{document}$\Sigma_{R_E}^{\mathrm{norm}}$\end{document} and ΣLE\begin{document}$\Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}$\end{document} can change depending on normalization of VLDA\begin{document}$V_{\mathrm{LDA}}$\end{document}. However, the difference is not important for the spatial correspondence between the two structures because they only affect scaling of the axes. Rather, ΛLDA′\begin{document}$\Lambda_{\mathrm{LDA}}^{\prime}$\end{document} and M~=(ΣLE)2\begin{document}$\tilde{M}= \left(\Sigma_{\mathrm{LE}}\right)^{2}$\end{document}, which determine the order of columns of VLDA\begin{document}$V_{\mathrm{LDA}}$\end{document} and V~rw\begin{document}$\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document}, are important.

sec.title: Application to main data
sec.p: The normalized coefficient proteome structure in Figure 6A and C is the scatterplots between different columns of BEnorm\begin{document}$B_{E}^{\mathrm{norm}}$\end{document}. The cosine similarity proteome structures in Figure 5L and Figure 6D are the scatterplots between different columns of V~rw\begin{document}$\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document}.

In our data analysis, we calculated V~rw\begin{document}$\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document} as V~rw=D−1/2V~sym\begin{document}$\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}=D^{-1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{sym}}$\end{document}, where each column of V~sym\begin{document}$\tilde{V}_{\rm sym}$\end{document} was normalized.

On the basis of the results of the mathematical analysis, we compared BEnorm\begin{document}$B_{E}^{\mathrm{norm}}$\end{document} and BEest,norm=(∑i=1ndi)1/2V~rw\begin{document}$B_{E}^{\mathrm{est, norm}}= \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}\right)^{1/2}\tilde{V}_{\mathrm{rw}}$\end{document} in Appendix 1—figure 9G.

The similarity between the projections of the two distinct omics structures onto low-dimensional subspaces suggests that Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} is close to the identity matrix (Equation 2.138 and Appendix 1—table 10). Appendix 1—figure 9A, C–E shows that the actual Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} is indeed significantly close to the identity matrix. This suggests that the major changes in cellular Raman spectra detectable by LDA reflect the major changes in the proteome characterized by LE based on stoichiometry balance (cosine similarity).

The structural similarity also suggests that directions of diag(b0)\begin{document}$\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right)$\end{document} and diag(P⊤P)1/2D\begin{document}$\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D$\end{document} are also similar (Equation 2.138 and Appendix 1—table 10).

In fact, Appendix 1—figure 9F confirmed good agreement between m1/2diag(b0)\begin{document}$m^{1/2}\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right)$\end{document} and (∑i=1ndi)−1/2diag(P⊤P)1/2D=diag(b0est)\begin{document}$\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}\right)^{-1/2}\mathrm{diag}\left(P^{\top}P\right)^{1/2}D =\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}\right)$\end{document}. Since these two quantities are calculated only from proteome data, this agreement is a characteristic of proteome data. See the next section (Section 2.2 in Appendix) for further analyses and discussion on this point.

sec.title: 2.2.1 From agreement between mb0\begin{document}$\sqrt{m}\,\boldsymbol{b}_{0}$\end{document} and b0est\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}$\end{document} to proportionality between L1\begin{document}$L^{1}$\end{document} norm/L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} norm ratio and degree
sec.p: We observed above that constant terms b0\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{b}_{0}$\end{document} and the estimated constant terms b0est\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}$\end{document} were strongly correlated (Appendix 1—figure 9F). It is of note that both b0\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{b}_{0}$\end{document} and b0est\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}$\end{document} can be calculated only from omics data. Specifically, from Equations 2.121 and 2.69,(2.140)b0∝((1m)⊤p1⋮(1m)⊤pn)=(∑i=1mpi1⋮∑i=1mpin)=(∑i=1m|pi1|⋮∑i=1m|pin|)=(‖p1‖1⋮‖pn‖1),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \boldsymbol{b}_{0} \propto\left(\begin{matrix}\left(\mathbf{1}_{m}\right)^{\top} \boldsymbol{p}_{1} \\\vdots \\\left(\mathbf{1}_{m}\right)^{\top} \boldsymbol{p}_{n}\end{matrix}\right)=\left(\begin{matrix}\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i 1} \\\vdots \\\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i n}\end{matrix}\right)=\left(\begin{matrix}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left|p_{i 1}\right| \\\vdots \\\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left|p_{i n}\right|\end{matrix}\right)=\left(\begin{matrix}\left\|\boldsymbol{p}_{1}\right\|_{1} \\\vdots \\\left\|\boldsymbol{p}_{n}\right\|_{1}\end{matrix}\right), $$\end{document}(2.141)b0est∝(d1(p1)⊤p1⋮dn(pn)⊤pn)=(d1∑i=1mpi12⋮dn∑i=1mpin2)=(d1‖p1‖2⋮dn‖pn‖2).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}} \propto\left(\begin{matrix}d_{1} \sqrt{\left(\boldsymbol{p}_{1}\right)^{\top} \boldsymbol{p}_{1}} \\\vdots \\d_{n} \sqrt{\left(\boldsymbol{p}_{n}\right)^{\top} \boldsymbol{p}_{n}}\end{matrix}\right)=\left(\begin{matrix}d_{1} \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i 1}^{2}} \\\vdots \\d_{n} \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_{i n}^{2}}\end{matrix}\right)=\left(\begin{matrix}d_{1}\left\|\boldsymbol{p}_{1}\right\|_{2} \\\vdots \\d_{n}\left\|\boldsymbol{p}_{n}\right\|_{2}\end{matrix}\right) . $$\end{document}

Here, ‖pj‖1\begin{document}$\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\right\| _{1}$\end{document} and ‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\right\| _{2}$\end{document} are the L1\begin{document}$L^{1}$\end{document} and L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} norms of pj\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{j}$\end{document} and reflect only the expression property of protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}. On the other hand, the degree dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} is a measure for the relationships of protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} with the other proteins because dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} is the sum of cosine similarities, dj=∑i=1ncos⁡θpipj\begin{document}$d_{j}= \sum_{i=1}^{n}\cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_i\boldsymbol{p}_j}$\end{document}.
The observed relation(2.142)mb0≈b0est\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{equation*}\sqrt{m}\,\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\approx \boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}\end{equation*}$$\end{document}

(Appendix 1—figure 9F) indicates that a proportionality relation(2.143)‖pj‖1‖pj‖2∝∑i=1ncos⁡θpipj\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\|_{1}}{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}}\|_{2}}\propto \sum_{i=1}^{n}\cos \theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_{i} \boldsymbol{p}_{j}}$$\end{document}
must hold approximately.

As mentioned in the main text, we refer to the ratio of L1\begin{document}$L^1$\end{document} norm to L2\begin{document}$L^2$\end{document} norm in the left-hand side of Equation 2.143 as expression generality score (gj\begin{document}$g_j$\end{document}) because it can be interpreted as a measure of constancy and generality of the expression levels of the protein (see ‘Interpretation of L1\begin{document}$L^1$\end{document} norm/L2\begin{document}$L^2$\end{document} norm ratio of an expression vector as a quantitative measure of expression generality’ in Materials and methods, Appendix 1—figure 8A and B).

When a protein is perfectly condition-specific and expressed only in a particular condition, its L1\begin{document}$L^{1}$\end{document} norm equals its L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} norm, and the ratio takes the minimum value one. When a protein is expressed equally across all the conditions, its L1\begin{document}$L^{1}$\end{document} norm is greater than its L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} norm and the ratio takes the maximum value, the square root of the number of conditions (Appendix 1—figure 8A and B). On the other hand, the right-hand side of Equation 2.143, which we refer to as stoichiometry conservation centrality (dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document}) in the main text, measures to what extent protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} conserves its stoichiometry with the other proteins. Therefore, the proportionality relation (Equation 2.143) suggests a global quantitative constraint between condition specificity of expression patterns and stoichiometry conservation strength. Positions of SCGs and density of genes in the csLE structure (Figure 5A, K, and L) already suggested that genes with less condition-specific expression patterns have more genes with stoichiometrically similar expression patterns, and the proportionality here quantitatively captures this property of omics dynamics. We remark that the proportionality relation was confirmed in all the omics data we analyzed in this paper (see Section 3.2 and Appendix 1—figure 7I–N).

sec.title: 2.2.2 Mathematics behind proportionality between stoichiometry conservation centrality dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} and expression generality score gj\begin{document}$g_{j}$\end{document}
sec.p: The cosine similarity-based analyses involve normalization of expression vectors pj\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{j}$\end{document} by its L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} norm ‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\right\| _{2}$\end{document} (Equation 2.77). Normalized expression vectors pj/‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\left.\boldsymbol{p}_{j}\big/\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\right\| _{2}\right.$\end{document} represent points on the first orthant division of a unit (m−1)\begin{document}$(m-1)$\end{document}-sphere in an m\begin{document}$m$\end{document}-dimensional space (∑i=1m(xi)2=1 (x1,…,xm≥0)\begin{document}$\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(x_{i}\right)^{2}= 1\ (x_{1}, \ldots, x_{m}\geq 0)$\end{document}). L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} normalization allows us to compare expression patterns without considering expression magnitudes, and an expression pattern is represented by a position on the unit (m−1)\begin{document}$(m-1)$\end{document}-sphere. Therefore, our cosine similarity-based quantification of stoichiometric balance in omics data is equivalent to evaluating distances (measured with angle) between points on the (m−1)\begin{document}$(m-1)$\end{document}-sphere.

Since stoichiometry conservation centrality dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} is the sum of cosine similarities,(2.144)dj=∑i=1ncos⁡θpjpi=∑i=1npj⋅pi‖pj‖2‖pi‖2\begin{document}$$\displaystyle d_{j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_j\boldsymbol{p}_i}=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_j\cdot\boldsymbol{p}_i}{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_2\|{\boldsymbol{p}_i}\|_2}$$\end{document}(2.145)=pj‖pj‖2⋅(∑i=1npi‖pi‖2).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\frac{\boldsymbol{p}_j}{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_2}\cdot \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_i}{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_i}\|_2}\right) .$$\end{document}
Defining(2.146)p~tot:=∑i=1npi‖pi‖2,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}:= \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{\boldsymbol{p}_i}{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_i}\|_2},$$\end{document}

we obtain(2.147)1∑i=1ndidj=pj‖pj‖2⋅p~tot‖p~tot‖2.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{1}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}}}\;d_{j}= \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_j}{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_2}\cdot \frac{\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_\mathrm{tot}}{\|{\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_\mathrm{tot}}\|_2}. $$\end{document}

Note that ‖p~tot‖2=∑i=1ndi\begin{document}$\|{\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_\mathrm{tot}}\|_{2}= \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}}$\end{document}. The last term p~tot/‖p~tot‖2\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}\big/\|{\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_\mathrm{tot}}\|_{2}$\end{document} is the normalized vector of the sum of all the normalized expression vectors, which we refer to as ‘expression-pattern norm vector’. Therefore, Equation 2.147 means that stoichiometry conservation centrality dj=∑icos⁡θpjpi\begin{document}$d_{j}= \sum_{i}\cos\theta_{\boldsymbol{p}_j\boldsymbol{p}_i}$\end{document} is proportional to the cosine of the angle between the expression-pattern norm vector p~tot/‖p~tot‖2\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}\big/\|{\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_\mathrm{tot}}\|_{2}$\end{document} and protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}’s expression pattern pj/‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{j}\big/\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_{2}$\end{document}. The more distant the expression pattern of a protein is from that specified by the expression-pattern norm vector, the smaller dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} is.

On the other hand, expression generality score gj\begin{document}$g_{j}$\end{document} is(2.148)gj=‖pj‖1‖pj‖2=‖pj‖pj‖2‖1\begin{document}$$\displaystyle g_{j} =\frac{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_1}{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_2}=\left\|{\frac{\boldsymbol{p}_j}{\left\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\right\| _2}}\right\| _{1}$$\end{document}(2.149)=pj‖pj‖2⋅1m.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle =\frac{\boldsymbol{p}_j}{\|\boldsymbol{p}_j\|_2}\cdot \boldsymbol{1}_m.$$\end{document}

Therefore,(2.150)1m‖pj‖1‖pj‖2=pj‖pj‖2⋅1mm.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{1}{\sqrt{m}}\,\frac{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_1}{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_2}= \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_j}{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_2}\cdot \frac{\boldsymbol{1}_m}{\sqrt{m}}. $$\end{document}

The last term 1m/m\begin{document}$\left. \boldsymbol{1}_{m}\big/ \sqrt{m}\right.$\end{document} is the normalized vector of 1m\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{1}_{m}$\end{document}, corresponding to the ‘center’ of the first orthant division of the unit (m−1)\begin{document}$(m-1)$\end{document}-sphere. In other words, 1m/m\begin{document}$\left. \boldsymbol{1}_{m}\big/ \sqrt{m}\right.$\end{document} represents ‘perfectly even expression pattern’ across conditions. Therefore, Equation 2.150 means that the expression generality score gj=‖pj‖1/‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\left. g_{j}= \|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_{1}\big/ \|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_{2}\right.$\end{document} is proportional to the cosine of the angle between the ‘perfectly even expression pattern’ 1m/m\begin{document}$\left. \boldsymbol{1}_{m}\big/ \sqrt{m}\right.$\end{document} and protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}’s expression pattern pj/‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\left. \boldsymbol{p}_{j}\big/ \|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_{2}\right.$\end{document}. The more distant the expression pattern of a protein is from the ‘perfectly even expression pattern’, the smaller the expression generality score is.

Comparing Equations 2.147 and 2.150, we see that if the expression-pattern norm vector p~tot/‖p~tot‖2\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}\big/ \|{\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_\mathrm{tot}}\|_{2}$\end{document} and the perfectly even expression pattern 1m/m\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{1}_{m}\big/ \sqrt{m}$\end{document} are equal, a proportional relationship(2.151)∀j, dj=∑i=1ndim‖pj‖1‖pj‖2\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \forall j,\ d_{j}= \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n d_i}{m}}\,\frac{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_1}{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_2}$$\end{document}
holds. Note that this is equivalent to mb0=b0est\begin{document}$\sqrt{m}\,\boldsymbol{b}_{0}= \boldsymbol{b}_{0}^{\mathrm{est}}$\end{document}.

Conversely, if p~tot/‖p~tot‖2\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}\big/ \|{\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_\mathrm{tot}}\|_{2}$\end{document} deviates from 1m/m\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{1}_{m}\big/ \sqrt{m}$\end{document}, the proportional relation between dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} and gj\begin{document}$g_{j}$\end{document} breaks. We found that the proteome data by Schmidt et al., 2016 showed p~tot/‖p~tot‖2≠1m/m\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}\big/ \|{\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_\mathrm{tot}}\|_{2} \neq \boldsymbol{1}_{m}\big/ \sqrt{m}$\end{document}. Instead, we found that the values of the elements of p~tot\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}$\end{document} increased approximately linearly with the population growth rates under corresponding conditions (Appendix 1—figure 8D). Such a strong positive correlation between the elements of p~tot\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}$\end{document} and the population growth rates is nontrivial and suggests a new growth law constraining the total of relative expression level changes of all the proteins.

Next, we consider the consequence of this positive correlation between the elements of p~tot\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}$\end{document} and the population growth rates. Let us consider the proteins whose expression generality score gj=‖pj‖1/‖pj‖2\begin{document}$g_{j}= \left. \|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_{1}\big/ \|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_{2}\right.$\end{document} takes the minimum value one. Namely, the expression of these proteins is completely condition-specific (Appendix 1—figure 8A and B). For such proteins, only one component of the expression pattern vector pj/‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{j}\big/ \|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_{2}$\end{document} is one, and the other components are zero. Thus, from Equation 2.147, their stoichiometry conservation centrality dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} becomes proportional to the elements of p~tot\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}$\end{document} corresponding to the conditions under which they are expressed. Since the values of the elements of p~tot\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}$\end{document} are positively correlated with the population growth rates, dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} of completely condition-specific proteins also exhibits a positive correlation with the growth rates under the conditions accompanying their expression (Appendix 1—figure 8C).
Such correlation can be confirmed for the proteins with nearly condition-specific expression patterns (PaaE, Asr, and DgoA in Figure 7B and C).

More generally, the deviation of dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} from the perfect proportionality line can be understood by the relation(2.152)dj∑i=1ndi−‖pj‖1m‖pj‖2=pj‖pj‖2⋅(p~tot‖p~tot‖2−1mm),\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{d_j}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}}}- \frac{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_1}{\sqrt{m}\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_2}= \frac{\boldsymbol{p}_j}{\|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_2}\cdot \left(\frac{\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_\mathrm{tot}}{\|{\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_\mathrm{tot}}\|_2}- \frac{\boldsymbol{1}_m}{\sqrt{m}}\right),$$\end{document}

which can be derived from Equations 2.147, 2.150. Note that the values of the elements of the last term p~tot/‖p~tot‖2−1m/m\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}\big/ \|{\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_\mathrm{tot}}\|_{2} - \boldsymbol{1}_{m}\big/ \sqrt{m}$\end{document} also increase with the population growth rates, being positive under fast growth conditions and negative under slow growth conditions (Appendix 1—figure 8D). Therefore, when protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} tends to be expressed higher under fast growth conditions, i.e., when the elements of pj/‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{p}_{j}\big/ \|{\boldsymbol{p}_j}\|_{2}$\end{document} corresponding to the fast growth conditions are relatively larger than those corresponding to the slow growth conditions, the left-hand side of Equation 2.152 becomes positive, and its dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} resides above the perfect proportionality line. On the other hand, when protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} tends to be expressed higher under slow growth conditions, its dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} resides below the perfect proportionality line.

In the gj\begin{document}$g_{j}$\end{document}-dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} plot in Figure 7A and B, we find several stretches of protein clusters above and below the perfect proportionality line. As expected from the argument above, each cluster corresponds to a group of proteins with similar expression patterns, and their positions relative to the proportionality line characterize the condition under which they are expressed the most (Figure 7C).

In summary, visualizing omics data by using the stoichiometry conservation centrality dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} and the expression generality score gj\begin{document}$g_{j}$\end{document} allows us to systematically characterize the condition-dependent expression pattern of each protein on the basis of its position in the plot. Interestingly, this systematic characterization of gene expression patterns (the relation between dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} and gj\begin{document}$g_{j}$\end{document}) was derived from our mathematical analyses of the correspondences between Raman and omics as we explained above.

sec.title: 3.1.1 Single-gene-level growth law
sec.p: Bacterial growth law states that the total abundances of ribosomal components increase linearly with growth rate (Neidhardt and Magasanik, 1960; Scott et al., 2010; Bremer and Dennis, 2008). The homeostatic core (the largest SCG) identified in our analysis contains many ribosomal proteins. Hence, it is plausible that the total abundance of homeostatic core proteins also increases linearly with growth rate, which we indeed found true (Appendix 1—figure 5A). Furthermore, the abundance ratios of homeostatic core proteins are conserved across conditions. Therefore, the intracellular abundance of each protein species in the homeostatic core is expected to increase linearly with growth rate.

Let pϵj\begin{document}$p_{\epsilon j}$\end{document} be the abundance of protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} in the homeostatic core in environment ϵ\begin{document}$\epsilon$\end{document}. Since this protein conserves the stoichiometry with the other homeostatic core proteins across conditions,(3.1)pϵipϵj=αij\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{p_{\epsilon i}}{p_{\epsilon j}}= \alpha_{ij}$$\end{document}

in any environments ϵ\begin{document}$\epsilon$\end{document} (αij\begin{document}$\alpha_{ij}$\end{document} is the environment-independent abundance ratio of the homeostatic core protein i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} to protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document}).

Let Mϵ=∑ipϵi=pϵj∑iαij\begin{document}$M_{\epsilon}= \sum_{i}p_{\epsilon i}= p_{\epsilon j}\sum_{i}\alpha_{ij}$\end{document} be the total abundance of homeostatic core proteins in environment ϵ\begin{document}$\epsilon$\end{document}. The growth law for the homeostatic core is(3.2)Mϵ=a+bgϵ,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle M_{\epsilon}= a + b g_{\epsilon}, $$\end{document}

where gϵ\begin{document}$g_{\epsilon}$\end{document} is the growth rate in environment ϵ\begin{document}$\epsilon$\end{document}, a\begin{document}$a$\end{document} is the y\begin{document}$y$\end{document}-intercept, and b\begin{document}$b$\end{document} is the slope of the linear relation. Therefore,(3.3)pϵj=a∑iαij+b∑iαijgϵ.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle p_{\epsilon j}= \frac{a}{\sum_{i}\alpha_{ij}}+ \frac{b}{\sum_{i}\alpha_{ij}}g_{\epsilon}.$$\end{document}
This shows that the abundance of homeostatic core proteins satisfies single-gene-level growth law.

sec.title: 3.1.2 Extended verification of stoichiometry conservation
sec.p: When the abundance ratios between protein i\begin{document}$i$\end{document} and protein j\begin{document}$j$\end{document} are conserved,(3.4)pcjpci=psjpsi,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{p_{cj}}{p_{ci}}= \frac{p_{sj}}{p_{si}},$$\end{document}

where c\begin{document}$c$\end{document} and s\begin{document}$s$\end{document} specify the environments (s\begin{document}$s$\end{document} signifies the standard environment). Hence,(3.5)pcjpsj=pcipsi=γc,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \frac{p_{cj}}{p_{sj}}= \frac{p_{ci}}{p_{si}}= \gamma_{c}, $$\end{document}

where γc\begin{document}$\gamma_{c}$\end{document} is the common abundance ratio of stoichiometry-conserving proteins with respect to the condition c\begin{document}$c$\end{document}. Note that γc\begin{document}$\gamma_{c}$\end{document} is common among all the proteins in a stoichiometry-conserving group.
From Equation 3.5,(3.6)log⁡pcj=log⁡psj+log⁡γc.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{equation*}\log p_{cj}= \log p_{sj}+ \log \gamma_{c}. \end{equation*}$$\end{document}

Therefore, plotting log⁡pcj\begin{document}$\log p_{cj}$\end{document} against log⁡psj\begin{document}$\log p_{sj}$\end{document} should find the stoichiometry-conserving proteins aligned on a straight line with a slope of 1. We indeed find such plots for the homeostatic core proteins (Appendix 1—figure 5B and C). This result confirms their stoichiometry conservation from a different perspective.

sec.title: 3.1.3 Linear dependence of common abundance ratio on growth rate
sec.p: Since the total amount of homeostatic core proteins increases linearly with growth rate (Appendix 1—figure 5A and Equation 3.2),(3.7)∑jpcj−∑jpsj=b(gc−gs).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \begin{equation*}\sum_{j}p_{cj}- \sum_{j}p_{sj}= b \left(g_{c}- g_{s}\right).\end{equation*}$$\end{document}

Since ∑jpcj=γc∑jpsj\begin{document}$\sum_{j}p_{cj}= \gamma_{c}\sum_{j}p_{sj}$\end{document},(3.8)(γc−1)∑jpsj=b(gc−gs).\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \left(\gamma_{c}-1 \right)\sum_{j}p_{sj}= b \left(g_{c}- g_{s}\right).$$\end{document}
Hence,(3.9)γc=(1−bgsMs)+bMsgc,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle \gamma_{c}= \left(1-\frac{b g_{s}}{M_{s}}\right) + \frac{b}{M_{s}}g_{c},$$\end{document}
where Ms=∑jpsj\begin{document}$M_{s}= \sum_{j}p_{sj}$\end{document}. Therefore, the common abundance ratio γc\begin{document}$\gamma_{c}$\end{document} also increases linearly with growth rate.

Estimating Γc:=log10⁡γc\begin{document}$\Gamma_{c} := \log_{10}\gamma_{c}$\end{document} as the y\begin{document}$y$\end{document}-intercepts of the regression lines with a slope of 1 (Appendix 1—figure 5B), we confirmed this linear dependence of common abundance ratio of homeostatic core proteins on growth rate (Appendix 1—figure 5D).

sec.title: Correspondence of three types of space
sec.p: The correspondences among LDA Raman, Raman-omics normalized coefficient omics structure, and csLE omics structure were also observed in other datasets. The datasets include Raman (this paper) and proteome (Schmidt et al., 2016) data of E. coli with different genotypes (BW25113, MG1655, and NCM3722) cultured in the ‘LB’ medium (Appendix 1—figure 11A–E) and in the ‘Glucose’ medium (Appendix 1—figure 11F–J), and Raman and transcriptome data of S. pombe cultured under 10 different environmental conditions (Appendix 1—figure 11K–O; Kobayashi-Kirschvink et al., 2018).

sec.title: Comparison of matrices obtained by mathematical analyses
sec.p: In addition to the comparison of three types of space, we also examined the matrices on the basis of results of the mathematical analyses (Appendix 1—table 10) using the aforementioned additional datasets; the closeness of BEnorm\begin{document}$B_{E}^{\mathrm{norm}}$\end{document} and BEest,norm\begin{document}$B_{E}^{\mathrm{est,norm}}$\end{document} (Appendix 1—figure 12F for Raman-proteome of E. coli with different genotypes cultured in ‘LB’, Appendix 1—figure 12L for Raman-proteome of E. coli with different genotypes cultured in ‘Glucose’, and Appendix 1—figure 12R for Raman-transcriptome of S. pombe cultured in 10 environment conditions), the closeness of Θ\begin{document}$\Theta$\end{document} to the identity matrix (Appendix 1—figure 12A–D for Raman-proteome of E. coli with different genotypes cultured in ‘LB’, Appendix 1—figure 12G–J for Raman-proteome of E. coli with different genotypes cultured in ‘Glucose’, and Appendix 1—figure 12M–P for Raman-transcriptome of S. pombe cultured in 10 environment conditions), and the correspondence between mdiag(b0)\begin{document}$\sqrt{m}\,\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right)$\end{document} and diag(b0est)\begin{document}$\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}^{\mathrm{est}}_{0}\right)$\end{document} (Appendix 1—figure 12E for Raman-proteome of E. coli with different genotypes cultured in ‘LB’, Appendix 1—figure 12K for Raman-proteome of E. coli with different genotypes cultured in ‘Glucose’, and Appendix 1—figure 12Q for Raman-transcriptome of S. pombe cultured under in 10 environment conditions).

We confirmed that the same results hold for these additional datasets. Note that the correspondence between mdiag(b0)\begin{document}$\sqrt{m}\, \mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right)$\end{document} and diag(b0est)\begin{document}$\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}^{\mathrm{est}}_{0}\right)$\end{document} does not involve Raman data. It is an intrinsic property of the omics data.

sec.title: Proportionality between expression generality and stoichiometry conservation centrality
sec.p: The correspondence between mdiag(b0)\begin{document}$\sqrt{m}\, \mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}_{0}\right)$\end{document} and diag(b0est)\begin{document}$\mathrm{diag}\left(\boldsymbol{b}^{\mathrm{est}}_{0}\right)$\end{document} suggested the proportionality between expression generality score gj\begin{document}$g_{j}$\end{document} and stoichiometry conservation centrality dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} in these omics data. We confirmed that the same results hold for these additional datasets (Appendix 1—figure 7I–N).

sec.title: Correlation between p~tot\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}$\end{document} and growth rates
sec.p: For the proteome data of E. coli with different genotypes (BW25113, MG1655, and NCM3722) cultured in ‘LB’ and in ‘Glucose’, growth rates were also reported (Schmidt et al., 2016). We confirmed a positive correlation between the elements of p~tot\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}$\end{document} and growth rates for these datasets (Appendix 1—figure 8E). See also the deviation from the proportionality line in the gj-dj\begin{document}$g_j \text{-} d_j$\end{document} plot (Appendix 1—figure 7I–J).

sec.title: Biological relevance of centrality of csLE structure
sec.p: We also confirmed centrality-essentiality correlation and centrality-evolutionary conservation correlation in the S. pombe transcriptome data (Appendix 1—figure 6B and E–G).

sec.title: Degree distribution and its destruction by randomization
sec.p: Degree (stoichiometry conservation centrality) distributions of csLE structure of the additional datasets also showed a similar pattern as the main data, and randomization of the omics data breaks the strong correlation of expression patterns in the actual data (Appendix 1—figure 7C, D, and H).

sec.title: 3.2.2 Additional datasets without Raman data
sec.p: Examining proteome structures with csLE does not require Raman data. Therefore, we additionally analyzed publicly available proteome data of M. tuberculosis and M. bovis under the growth conditions with distinct oxygen levels (Schubert et al., 2015), and the proteome data of S. cerevisiae under various environmental conditions (Lahtvee et al., 2017).

We characterized csLE structures of these datasets (Appendix 1—figure 13). Furthermore, we confirmed the proportionality between expression generality score gj\begin{document}$g_{j}$\end{document} and stoichiometry conservation centrality dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} (Appendix 1—figure 7K–M). For the proteome data of S. cerevisiae, growth rates were also reported (Lahtvee et al., 2017). The S. cerevisiae cells were cultured in chemostat at the same dilution rate in any condition. In fact, we observed little variation of p~tot\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}$\end{document} (Appendix 1—figure 8F), which leads to little deviation from the proportionality line (Appendix 1—figure 7M).

Degree (stoichiometry conservation centrality) distributions of csLE structure of the additional datasets also showed a similar pattern as the main data, and randomization of the omics data breaks the strong correlation of expression patterns in the actual data (Appendix 1—figure 7E–G).

caption.title: Schematic illustration of the approach in this study.
caption.p: Related to Figure 1. Raman spectra and gene expression profiles are both high-dimensional vectors and can be represented as points in high-dimensional spaces. Coarse-graining Raman spectra by dimensional reduction finds condition-dependent differences in their global spectral patterns (see Figure 2). The dimension-reduced spectra were linked to and used to predict condition-dependent global gene expression profiles (see Figure 2), which implies that global changes in spectral patterns detect differences in cellular physiological states. The analysis of this linkage led us to discover a stoichiometry-conserving constraint on gene expression, which enabled us to represent gene expression profiles in a functionally relevant low-dimensional space (i; see also Figures 3—5). Then, we find a nontrivial correspondence between these low-dimensional Raman and gene expression spaces (ii; see also Figure 6). This correspondence provides an omics-level interpretation of global Raman spectral patterns and a quantitative constraint between expression generality and stoichiometry conservation centrality (ii; see also Figure 7, Appendix 1—figure 9).

caption.title: Custom-built Raman microscope and analyses of E. coli Raman spectra.
caption.p: Related to Figure 2. (A) Schematic diagram of the Raman microscope used in this study. (B) Representative Raman spectra from single E. coli cells. The fingerprint region of one spectrum is shown for each condition. (C) Linear superposition of Raman shifts. Each linear discriminant analysis (LDA) axis is a linear superposition of Raman shifts. These figures show the coefficients for LDA1 (left) and LDA2 (right). (D) Relationship between Raman LDA1 axis and growth rates. The horizontal axis represents Raman LDA1 axis. The vertical axis represents growth rates measured in Schmidt et al., 2016. Each point corresponds to the data for one condition. Pearson correlation coefficient is 0.81±0.09.

caption.title: Estimation of proteomes from Raman spectra.
caption.p: Related to Figure 2. Comparing the measured proteomes with those estimated from Raman spectra. The horizontal and vertical axes represent the estimated and measured proteomes, respectively. Proteins with negative estimated abundance are not shown in these figures. The conditions with the largest and the second largest numbers of proteins with negative estimated abundance were ‘stationary3days’ (666 proteins) and ‘LB’ (359 proteins). The conditions with the fewest and the second fewest negatively estimated proteins were ‘GlucosepH6’ (0 proteins) and ‘Xylose’ (7 proteins).

caption.title: Comparison of stoichiometry conservation among Clusters of Orthologous Group (COG) classes.
caption.p: Related to Figure 3. (A and B) Relations between protein abundance and constant terms of Raman-proteome coefficients. The horizontal axes are b0\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{b}_{0}$\end{document} (constant terms), and the vertical axes are p^i⊤\begin{document}$\hat{\boldsymbol{p}}_{i}^{\top}$\end{document} (protein abundance). Dashed lines are the least squares regression lines with intercept zero for information storage and processing (ISP) COG class members. The average of B−iest\begin{document}$B_{-i}^{\mathrm{est}}$\end{document} was used as an estimate of B\begin{document}$B$\end{document} here. In (A), only ISP COG class members are shown for three representative conditions: ‘Galactose’, ‘Glucose’, and ‘GlycerolAA’. In (B), all proteins are shown for a representative condition, ‘GlycerolAA’. (C) Relations between protein abundance and growth rates of E. coli under 15 environmental conditions. We analyzed the absolute quantitative proteome data, growth rate data, and COG annotation reported by Schmidt et al., 2016. Lines represent different protein species. Error bars are standard errors. The top panel is for the Cellular Processes and Signaling COG class; the middle is for the ISP COG class; and the bottom is for the Metabolism COG class. (D) Relations between protein abundance and growth rates of three E. coli strains (BW25113, MG1655, and NCM3722) under two culture conditions. We again analyzed the data by Schmidt et al., 2016. Lines represent different protein species. Error bars are standard errors. (E and F) COG class-dependent expression pattern similarity of E. coli proteomes between conditions. The E. coli proteome data under the 15 different environmental conditions were analyzed. The similarity is evaluated by Pearson correlation coefficients of log expression levels in (E) and by cosine similarity in (F). We consider all the combinations of the 15 conditions. Thus, there are 105 data points for each COG class. The box-and-whisker plots summarize the distributions of the points. The lines inside the boxes denote the medians. The top and bottom edges of the boxes denote the 25th percentiles and 75th percentiles, respectively. Note that (E) and (F) are evaluations of the same data used in Figure 3B in the main text with different similarity indices. (G) COG class-dependent expression pattern similarity between different strains of E. coli (BW25113, MG1655, and NCM3722). The absolute quantitative proteome data and COG annotation were taken from Schmidt et al., 2016. The similarity was evaluated by cosine similarity. The data contain three strains. Thus, there are three points for each COG class. The top panel is for the ‘Glucose’ condition, and the bottom is for the ‘LB’ condition. (H–J) COG class-dependent expression pattern similarity in other organisms. (H) is for M. tuberculosis (data from Schubert et al., 2015; six environmental conditions [time points]), (I) for M. bovis (data from Schubert et al., 2015; six environmental conditions [time points]), and (J) for S. cerevisiae (data from Lahtvee et al., 2017; 10 environmental conditions). The COG annotations were taken from the December 2014 release of 2003-2014 COGs (Galperin et al., 2015) and the Release 3 of ‘Mycobrowser’ (Kapopoulou et al., 2011) for (H) and (I) and from the Comprehensive Sake Yeast Genome Database (S288C strain) (Akao et al., 2011) for (J). The unit for protein abundance was fg/cell for (H) and (I) and fg in pg dry cell weight for (J).

caption.title: Single-gene-level growth law in the homeostatic core.
caption.p: Related to Figure 4. (A) Relationship between population growth rates and total abundance of SCG 1 (homeostatic core) proteins. Here, we analyzed the E. coli proteome data (Schmidt et al., 2016), focusing on the 15 conditions for which we obtained Raman data. The dashed line is the least squares regression line. (B) Scatterplots of log abundance of SCG 1 (homeostatic core) proteins. Here, the proteomes under three representative conditions, ‘LB’, ‘Glucose’, and ‘Galactose’, are compared with that under the standard condition ‘Glycerol’. Each colored line is the linear regression line with slope one for the points with the same color. The vertical line is x=0\begin{document}$x=0$\end{document}. (C) Relationship between population growth rate and coefficient of determination of linear regression in (B). The vertical line represents the growth rate under the standard condition (‘Glycerol’). (D) Linear relationship between common abundance ratio and growth rates. The vertical axis represents 10Γc\begin{document}$10^{\Gamma_c}$\end{document}, where Γc\begin{document}$\Gamma_{c}$\end{document} is the y-intercepts in (B) (see Section 3.1.2 in Appendix). The dashed line is the linear regression line. The horizontal line is y=1\begin{document}$y=1$\end{document}, and the x\begin{document}$x$\end{document} coordinate of the vertical line is the growth rate under the standard condition (‘Glycerol’). (E) The gene loci of the proteins belonging to the condition-specific stoichiometrically conserved groups (SCGs) on the chromosome (ASM75055v1.46; Howe et al., 2020). Colored dots are nodes (genes), and gray lines are edges (high cosine similarity relationships). The edge in the map of SCG 5 cannot be seen because their gene loci are clustered in close proximity in the same operon.

caption.title: Functional relevance of stoichiometry conservation centrality.
caption.p: Related to Figure 5. (A) Relationship between gene essentiality and stoichiometry conservation centrality in E. coli. The proportion of essential genes is plotted for each stoichiometry conservation centrality rank range. In this plot, we calculated stoichiometry conservation centrality based on the E. coli proteome data (Schmidt et al., 2016) under the 15 conditions for which we obtained Raman data. The list of essential genes was downloaded from EcoCyc (Keseler et al., 2017). (B) Relationship between gene essentiality and stoichiometry conservation centrality in S. pombe. We calculated stoichiometry conservation centrality based on the S. pombe transcriptome data reported in Kobayashi-Kirschvink et al., 2018. Only coding genes are considered in this plot, though stoichiometry conservation centrality values were calculated using both coding and non-coding genes. Gene classification is based on PomBase (Harris et al., 2022). Some bins do not reach 100% in sum because 11 coding genes in the S. pombe transcriptome data were not found in the current PomBase. (C) Relationship between ratio of coding genes and stoichiometry conservation centrality in the S. pombe transcriptome data. The coding/non-coding assignment is based on PomBase (Harris et al., 2022). (D) Correlation between stoichiometry conservation and evolutionary conservation. In this plot, we calculated stoichiometry conservation centrality based on the E. coli proteome data (Schmidt et al., 2016) under the 15 conditions for which we obtained Raman data. Colors represent the height of each bar. The distributions of stoichiometry conservation centrality were compared between the top 25% and the bottom 25% fractions in the number of orthologs rankings. The fraction with many orthologs tends to have higher stoichiometry conservation centrality (one-sided Brunner-Munzel test, p=7.84×10−15\begin{document}$p = 7.84\times10^{-15}$\end{document}). The distributions of the number of orthologs were compared between the top 25% and the bottom 25% stoichiometry conservation centrality fractions. The high centrality fraction tends to have more orthologs (one-sided Brunner-Munzel test, p=1.46×10−11\begin{document}$p = 1.46\times10^{-11}$\end{document}). Ortholog data were taken from OrthoMCL-DB (Chen et al., 2006). (E–G) Correlation between stoichiometry conservation and evolutionary conservation in S. pombe. We calculated stoichiometry conservation centrality based on the S. pombe transcriptome data reported in Kobayashi-Kirschvink et al., 2018. In (E), the result is shown by a two-dimensional histogram. Colors represent the height of each bar. The distributions of the number of orthologs were compared between the top 25% and the bottom 25% stoichiometry conservation centrality fractions. The high centrality fraction tends to have more orthologs (one-sided Brunner-Munzel test, p=0.00548\begin{document}$p = 0.00548$\end{document}). The direct comparison between the two fractions is shown in (F). The distributions of stoichiometry conservation centrality were compared between the top 25% and the bottom 25% fractions in the number of orthologs rankings. The fraction with many orthologs tends to have higher stoichiometry conservation centrality (one-sided Brunner-Munzel test, p=0.00270\begin{document}$p = 0.00270$\end{document}). The direct comparison between the two fractions is shown in (G). Ortholog data were taken from OrthoMCL-DB (Chen et al., 2006). (H) Applying principal component analysis (PCA) to L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document}-normalized proteomes. PCA (with mean centering) was applied to L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document}-normalized proteome data [p1/‖p1‖2⋯pn/‖pn‖2]\begin{document}$\left[\begin{array}{lll}\left. \boldsymbol{p}_{1} \big/\left\|\boldsymbol{p}_{1}\right\|_{2}\right. & \cdots & \left.\boldsymbol{p}_{n} \big/\left\|\boldsymbol{p}_{n}\right\|_{2}\right.\end{array}\right]$\end{document}. Here, we analyzed the E. coli proteome data under the 15 conditions for which we obtained Raman data. The left is a projection onto a two-dimensional space, and the right is a projection onto a three-dimensional space. The axes for visualization were selected by considering similarity to the cosine similarity LE (csLE) structure.

caption.title: Distributions and constraints with respect to stoichiometry conservation centrality (degree).
caption.p: Related to Figure 5 and Figure 7. (A) Comparison of degree (stoichiometry conservation centrality) distributions between original (yellow) and randomized (blue) E. coli proteome data. We created randomized proteome data by shuffling the expression levels across the protein species within each condition. We used the E. coli proteome data (Schmidt et al., 2016) under the 15 conditions for which we obtained Raman data. (B) Comparison of the gj\begin{document}$g_{j}$\end{document}-dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} relationships between original (yellow) and randomized data (blue). The horizontal axis is expression generality score (gj=\begin{document}$g_{j}=$\end{document}L1\begin{document}$L^{1}$\end{document} norm/L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} norm), and the vertical axis is stoichiometry conservation centrality (dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document}: degree). Each dot represents a protein species. The dashed lines are y=n\begin{document}$y = n$\end{document}, x=1,m\begin{document}$x = 1, \sqrt{m}$\end{document} (n=2058,m=15\begin{document}$n = 2058, m = 15$\end{document}). The solid lines are y=∑idi/m x\begin{document}$y=\sqrt{\left. \sum_{i}d_{i}\big/ m \right.}\ x$\end{document}. (C–H) Degree (stoichiometry conservation centrality) distributions for additional datasets. Yellow histograms are for the original data, and blue histograms are for the randomized data. (C) For the proteomes of three E. coli strains (BW25113, MG1655, and NCM3722) in LB (Schmidt et al., 2016); (D) for the proteomes of the three E. coli strains in M9 Glucose (Schmidt et al., 2016); (E) for the proteomes of M. tuberculosis (Schubert et al., 2015); (F) for the proteomes of M. bovis (Schubert et al., 2015); (G) for the proteomes of S. cerevisiae (Lahtvee et al., 2017); and (H) for the transcriptomes of S. pombe (Kobayashi-Kirschvink et al., 2018). (I–N) gj\begin{document}$g_{j}$\end{document}-dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} relationships for additional datasets. Each gray dot represents a protein species. The proteins belonging to the homeostatic core in each dataset are shown in magenta; those belonging to condition-specific stoichiometrically conserved groups (SCGs) are indicated in different colors in each plot. See the caption of Appendix 1—figures 11 and 13 for the cosine similarity threshold to specify the homeostatic core and the condition-specific SCGs in each dataset. The dashed lines are y=n,x=1,m\begin{document}$y = n, x = 1, \sqrt{m}$\end{document}. The solid lines through the origins are y=∑i=1ndi/mx\begin{document}$y=\sqrt{\left. \sum_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}\big/m \right.}\, x$\end{document} (I) for the proteomes of the three E. coli strains in LB (Schmidt et al., 2016); (J) for the proteomes of the three E. coli strains in M9 Glucose (Schmidt et al., 2016); (K) for the proteomes of M. tuberculosis (Schubert et al., 2015); (L) for the proteomes of M. bovis (Schubert et al., 2015) (M) for the proteomes of S. cerevisiae (Lahtvee et al., 2017); and (N) for the transcriptomes of S. pombe (Kobayashi-Kirschvink et al., 2018).

caption.title: Properties of normalized expression vectors.
caption.p: Related to Figure 7. (A and B) Schematic explanation for the interpretation of the L1\begin{document}$L^{1}$\end{document} norm/L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} norm ratio of expression vectors as an index of expression generality. (A) is a two-dimensional case, and (B) is a three-dimensional case. The inset in (A) schematically explains L1\begin{document}$L^{1}$\end{document} norm and L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} norm of an expression vector. See ‘Interpretation of L1\begin{document}$L^1$\end{document} norm/L2\begin{document}$L^2$\end{document} norm ratio of an expression vector as a quantitative measure of expression generality’ in Materials and methods for details. (C) Schematic explanation for deviations of points from the proportionality line in the gj\begin{document}$g_{j}$\end{document}-dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} plots. Here, we consider four condition-specific protein species a, b, c, and d labeled in the descending order of growth rates under the conditions accompanying their expression. Note that their L1\begin{document}$L^{1}$\end{document} norm/L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document} norm ratios are all one on the horizontal axis. One can show that the degree (stoichiometry conservation centrality) dj\begin{document}$d_{j}$\end{document} is proportional to the inner product of L2\begin{document}$L^{2}$\end{document}-normalized expression vector pj/‖pj‖2\begin{document}$\left. \boldsymbol{p}_{j}\big/ \|{\boldsymbol{p}_{j}\|_2}\right.$\end{document} and the expression norm vector p~tot/‖p~tot‖2\begin{document}$\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_{\mathrm{tot}}\big/ \|{\tilde{\boldsymbol{p}}_\mathrm{tot}}\|_{2}$\end{document} (see Equation 2.147 in Section 2.2.2).
